label: Phase 2 Questions — Core Chronic Disease Management, Part B (np-p2-119 through np-p2-178)
difficultyPolicy: hard or expert only — no medium

questions.stemAnchor: 45-year-old + TSH 12.4 + free T4 normal + asymptomatic — subclinical hypothyroidism treatment threshold
questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents for an annual well visit. She reports no symptoms. TSH is 12.4 mIU/L (elevated). Free T4 is 1.0 ng/dL (normal). Anti-TPO antibodies are positive at 240 IU/mL. She has no personal or family history of thyroid disease. She is currently trying to conceive. Which of the following is the most appropriate management?

options.A: No treatment — TSH <20 mIU/L in an asymptomatic patient does not require levothyroxine
options.B: Initiate levothyroxine — TSH >10 mIU/L plus positive anti-TPO antibodies and desire for pregnancy are all independent indications for treatment of subclinical hypothyroidism
options.C: Repeat TSH in 6 months — subclinical hypothyroidism always resolves spontaneously
options.D: Start methimazole — anti-TPO antibodies indicate Graves disease requiring antithyroid treatment

questions.rationale: Subclinical hypothyroidism (SCH) treatment thresholds: (1) TSH >10 mIU/L in any patient = treat (regardless of symptoms — higher TSH levels carry increased CV risk and symptom risk); (2) TSH 5–10 + symptoms = consider treating; (3) TSH 5–10 + positive anti-TPO antibodies = consider treating (higher risk of progression to overt hypothyroidism); (4) Pregnancy intent or pregnancy = treat even with lower TSH elevations (target TSH <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester). This patient meets multiple criteria: TSH 12.4 (>10), positive anti-TPO, and attempting to conceive. Treatment is clearly indicated. Anti-TPO antibodies (option D) indicate Hashimoto thyroiditis (the most common cause of primary hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient countries) — not Graves disease (which causes hyperthyroidism via TSI antibodies). SF-02 slugA scope is maintained: this question covers the decision to treat, not dosing specifics.

questions.stemAnchor: Suppressed TSH 0.04 + elevated free T4 + exophthalmos + diffuse goiter — Graves disease vs toxic adenoma
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman presents with 3-month history of weight loss (12 lbs), heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, and anxiety. She also reports her eyes have been 'bulging.' On exam: diffuse thyroid enlargement (smooth, non-nodular), exophthalmos bilaterally, fine tremor, warm moist skin. TSH <0.01 mIU/L, free T4 4.2 ng/dL (elevated), total T3 340 ng/dL (elevated). Which of the following diagnostic distinction is most important and what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Hashimoto thyroiditis with transient thyrotoxicosis (Hashitoxicosis) — this resolves spontaneously without treatment
options.B: Graves disease — the combination of diffuse goiter + exophthalmos + thyroid stimulating immunoglobulin (TSI) antibodies distinguishes it from toxic nodular goiter
options.C: Toxic multinodular goiter — the diffuse enlargement is characteristic of toxic nodular disease
options.D: Pituitary TSH-secreting adenoma — TSH would be elevated, not suppressed, in this condition

questions.rationale: Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism. Pathognomonic features: (1) diffuse smooth goiter (not nodular), (2) Graves ophthalmopathy (exophthalmos/proptosis — unique to Graves, caused by autoimmune retroorbital inflammation), (3) Graves dermopathy (pretibial myxedema — rare but pathognomonic if present). TSI (thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulin) antibodies are diagnostic. Hashitoxicosis (option A) occurs in early Hashimoto — is a transient destructive thyroiditis phase, typically mild and without exophthalmos. Toxic multinodular goiter (option C) has a nodular goiter, not diffuse, and no ophthalmopathy. TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (option D) would cause ELEVATED TSH with elevated T4 — not suppressed TSH. Treatment (radioactive iodine, antithyroid drugs, or thyroidectomy) belongs to endocrinology referral.

questions.stemAnchor: Thyroid nodule 1.8 cm + TSH normal + ultrasound hypoechoic + microcalcifications — FNAB indication and cancer workup
questions.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents after her internist palpated a right thyroid nodule during a routine exam. Ultrasound reveals: right thyroid nodule 1.8 cm, hypoechoic, irregular margins, 3 microcalcifications, taller-than-wide shape. TSH is 1.8 mIU/L (normal). She is asymptomatic. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Repeat ultrasound in 12 months — most thyroid nodules are benign
options.B: Order thyroid scintigraphy (radioactive iodine scan) — a hot nodule rules out malignancy
options.C: Refer for fine-needle aspiration biopsy (FNAB) — this nodule has high-risk sonographic features that meet the threshold for tissue diagnosis
options.D: Start levothyroxine to suppress TSH and shrink the nodule

questions.rationale: High-risk sonographic features (per ATA/ACR TI-RADS guidelines) that indicate FNAB for nodules ≥1 cm: hypoechogenicity, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide shape, and extrathyroidal extension — this nodule has FOUR high-risk features (hypoechoic, irregular margins, microcalcifications, taller-than-wide). FNAB is clearly indicated. Papillary thyroid carcinoma (most common thyroid cancer) characteristically shows these ultrasound features. Thyroid scintigraphy (option B) is indicated when TSH is SUPPRESSED (to evaluate a hot autonomous nodule, which is virtually always benign) — TSH is normal here. TSH suppression with levothyroxine (option D) to shrink nodules is no longer recommended — it has minimal efficacy and exposes patients to exogenous hyperthyroidism risk. Observation alone (option A) is inappropriate for high-risk sonographic features.

questions.stemAnchor: 55-year-old + secondary hypothyroidism + TSH low + free T4 low + pituitary MRI — central hypothyroidism diagnosis
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance over 6 months. He had transphenoidal resection of a pituitary macroadenoma 18 months ago. Lab results: TSH 0.8 mIU/L (low-normal), free T4 0.4 ng/dL (low — reference 0.8–1.8 ng/dL). Which of the following best explains this pattern and most appropriate next step?

options.A: Primary hypothyroidism — elevated TSH is the hallmark; the TSH of 0.8 rules out hypothyroidism
options.B: Central (secondary) hypothyroidism — pituitary dysfunction causes inappropriately low/normal TSH with low free T4; initiate levothyroxine titrated to free T4 (not TSH target)
options.C: Euthyroid sick syndrome — the TSH of 0.8 with low T4 is a normal finding post-surgery
options.D: Pituitary tumor recurrence — repeat MRI is the only appropriate next step; do not treat until diagnosis confirmed

questions.rationale: Central (secondary or tertiary) hypothyroidism: caused by pituitary (secondary) or hypothalamic (tertiary) disease — the TSH is inappropriately low or normal-low despite low free T4. This is the opposite of primary hypothyroidism where TSH is ELEVATED. In primary hypothyroidism: elevated TSH + low free T4. In central hypothyroidism: TSH inappropriately low/normal + low free T4. Key point: TSH is NOT a reliable monitoring parameter in central hypothyroidism — use free T4 to guide levothyroxine dosing (target mid-normal range for free T4). This patient had pituitary surgery — the most common cause of central hypothyroidism in adults. TSH alone (option A) would have missed this diagnosis. MRI can assess for recurrence (option D) but should not delay thyroid replacement.

questions.stemAnchor: 58-year-old + TSH elevated + new atrial fibrillation + levothyroxine recently started — overtreatment iatrogenic hyperthyroidism
questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman was started on levothyroxine 150 mcg daily 3 months ago for primary hypothyroidism (baseline TSH 18.4). At her 3-month follow-up, TSH is now 0.08 mIU/L (suppressed). She presents with new palpitations and is found to have atrial fibrillation on ECG. She reports intermittent heat intolerance and anxiety over the past month. Which of the following is the most likely explanation and appropriate action?

options.A: Her levothyroxine dose is correct — TSH suppression is the therapeutic target in hypothyroidism
options.B: The dose of 150 mcg is excessive — iatrogenic hyperthyroidism (suppressed TSH) can precipitate AF, osteoporosis, and cardiac arrhythmias; reduce the levothyroxine dose and recheck TSH in 6 weeks
options.C: The AF is unrelated to levothyroxine — start beta-blocker and continue current levothyroxine dose
options.D: Add methimazole to counteract levothyroxine-induced hyperthyroidism

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine overtreatment (iatrogenic hyperthyroidism): suppressed TSH (<0.1 mIU/L) from excessive levothyroxine is associated with atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis/fractures, cardiac hypertrophy, and anxiety. The new AF is directly attributable to the suppressed TSH in this context. Treatment goal for hypothyroidism: TSH within the NORMAL range (0.5–4.5 mIU/L for most adults; 0.5–2.0 for younger adults; up to 6.0 acceptable in elderly ≥70). TSH suppression is the target ONLY in thyroid cancer post-thyroidectomy — not in routine hypothyroidism. Action: reduce levothyroxine dose (e.g., from 150 to 112 mcg or 125 mcg), rate-control the AF (beta-blocker), and recheck TSH + fT4 at 6–8 weeks. Adding methimazole (option D) would create antagonism and is not the approach — simply reduce the exogenous source.

questions.stemAnchor: Hypothyroid patient + new depression + inadequate response to antidepressant — check TSH before escalating psychiatric treatment
questions.stem: A 44-year-old woman with major depressive disorder has been on sertraline 200 mg daily for 4 months with only partial response (PHQ-9 improved from 22 to 14). Her psychiatrist asks you to add an augmentation agent. She has gained 15 pounds over the past year, feels cold all the time, and reports significant fatigue beyond what she attributes to depression. On review, no thyroid function tests have been ordered in 2 years. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step before escalating psychiatric medication?

options.A: Add aripiprazole 5 mg as augmentation — this is the evidence-based next step for partial response to sertraline
options.B: Switch from sertraline to duloxetine — SNRIs are more effective than SSRIs for treatment-resistant depression
options.C: Order TSH — undiagnosed or undertreated hypothyroidism can mimic and worsen depression and may be responsible for the partial antidepressant response
options.D: Add bupropion — it will both augment antidepressant response and help with the weight gain

questions.rationale: Before escalating psychiatric treatment for partial antidepressant response, it is essential to exclude treatable medical causes of depression and fatigue. This patient has multiple hypothyroid symptoms (cold intolerance, weight gain, fatigue, cognitive slowing) in addition to her depression. Undiagnosed hypothyroidism can cause depression that is completely treatment-resistant to antidepressants until the thyroid is treated. Additionally, undertreated hypothyroidism (normal TSH 2 years ago but now potentially elevated) is a common and easily correctable cause of antidepressant non-response. The correct action is TSH before adding a second psychiatric medication. If hypothyroidism is found and treated, the depression may improve significantly without psychiatric medication escalation. This is a classic NP integrative medicine/primary care decision point — the intersection of SF-04 (MDD) and SF-02 (thyroid) scope fences.

questions.stemAnchor: Hypothyroid patient + calcium supplement co-administration + TSH rising despite good dose — absorption interaction identification
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with primary hypothyroidism has been stable on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily for 2 years with TSH consistently around 1.8 mIU/L. At today's visit, TSH is 6.4 mIU/L and she is feeling more fatigued. She has not changed her levothyroxine dose. Medication review reveals she started calcium carbonate 1500 mg daily 3 months ago (for osteoporosis prevention) at the recommendation of her orthopedist. She takes both medications at the same time each morning. Which of the following best explains the TSH rise?

options.A: Calcium carbonate is directly suppressing TSH production in the pituitary
options.B: Calcium carbonate reduces levothyroxine absorption when taken simultaneously — counsel to take levothyroxine 4 hours before or after calcium
options.C: Her hypothyroidism has progressed and requires a dose increase regardless of timing
options.D: Calcium carbonate and levothyroxine have no clinically meaningful drug interaction

questions.rationale: Calcium carbonate (and calcium citrate) significantly reduces levothyroxine absorption when taken simultaneously — calcium binds to thyroxine in the GI tract and reduces bioavailability by up to 20–40%. This is a well-documented, clinically significant absorption interaction. The TSH rise (1.8 → 6.4) over 3 months precisely coincides with starting the calcium supplement co-administration. Correction: counsel the patient to take levothyroxine 4 hours apart from calcium supplements. After correcting the timing, recheck TSH in 6–8 weeks — the TSH should return to baseline without any dose change. Other absorption inhibitors (all require 4-hour separation): iron supplements, PPIs, sucralfate, cholestyramine. This is a high-yield SF-02 slugB topic: absorption interactions with levothyroxine.

questions.stemAnchor: 75-year-old + hypothyroid + angina history + levothyroxine starting dose — cardiac-sparing low-dose initiation
questions.stem: A 75-year-old man with stable angina and newly diagnosed overt primary hypothyroidism (TSH 22 mIU/L, fT4 0.3 ng/dL) asks about starting levothyroxine. He has had a 30-year history of ischemic heart disease and has had stable angina for 3 years. Which of the following represents the most appropriate levothyroxine starting strategy?

options.A: Calculate full replacement dose (1.6 mcg/kg/day) and start immediately
options.B: Start at 25 mcg daily and titrate by 12.5–25 mcg increments every 6–8 weeks, monitoring for angina exacerbation
options.C: Delay levothyroxine treatment until coronary artery bypass is performed — thyroid hormone is contraindicated with active coronary disease
options.D: Start at 200 mcg daily to rapidly correct the severe hypothyroidism and prevent myxedema coma

questions.rationale: In elderly patients and those with established ischemic heart disease or cardiac risk, levothyroxine must be started at a LOW dose (25–50 mcg daily) with SLOW titration (12.5–25 mcg increments every 6–8 weeks). Rationale: even mild increases in metabolic rate and heart rate from thyroid hormone can increase myocardial oxygen demand, precipitating or worsening angina, arrhythmia, or MI in patients with ischemic heart disease. Full replacement (1.6 mcg/kg/day) is appropriate for healthy adults without cardiac disease. Starting at 200 mcg (option D) would rapidly precipitate angina or ACS. Delaying treatment (option C) is inappropriate — severe hypothyroidism (TSH 22, low fT4) impairs cardiac function and increases CV risk; treatment with careful titration is safer than delay. Recheck TSH at 6–8 weeks after EACH dose adjustment.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient on levothyroxine + rifampin started for TB — dose adjustment required
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with primary hypothyroidism (well-controlled on levothyroxine 125 mcg daily, TSH 2.1 mIU/L) is diagnosed with active pulmonary tuberculosis and started on RIPE therapy (rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, ethambutol). At her 8-week follow-up, TSH is now 8.9 mIU/L and she reports return of fatigue and cold intolerance. She has been taking both medications as prescribed. Which of the following best explains the TSH rise and appropriate management?

options.A: TB medications always cause hypothyroidism; add another thyroid medication
options.B: Rifampin induces CYP enzymes that increase levothyroxine hepatic metabolism and clearance, requiring a levothyroxine dose increase; recheck TSH after dose adjustment
options.C: Isoniazid directly blocks thyroid hormone synthesis; discontinue isoniazid
options.D: The TSH rise represents the immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome; no thyroid adjustment is needed

questions.rationale: Rifampin is a potent inducer of CYP450 enzymes (particularly CYP1A2, CYP2C9, CYP3A4) — it dramatically increases the hepatic metabolism and clearance of many drugs including levothyroxine. The result: levothyroxine is metabolized faster → lower plasma T4 levels → pituitary senses low T4 → increases TSH production. Management: increase levothyroxine dose (often requires 25–50% increase) while on rifampin; recheck TSH at 6–8 weeks after adjustment. This drug interaction must be anticipated whenever rifampin is initiated in a hypothyroid patient. Other CYP-inducing drugs requiring levothyroxine dose increase: phenytoin, carbamazepine. Isoniazid (option C) has not been shown to block thyroid synthesis directly. This is a high-yield SF-02 slugB drug interaction question.

questions.stemAnchor: Hypothyroid woman + pregnancy confirmed + levothyroxine dose increase timing — 30% dose increase immediately
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with known primary hypothyroidism (TSH 1.9 mIU/L on levothyroxine 88 mcg daily) presents excited to report a positive home pregnancy test. She is approximately 5 weeks pregnant by her last menstrual period. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action regarding her thyroid management?

options.A: Continue current levothyroxine dose — TSH 1.9 is within normal range and no change is needed until the second trimester
options.B: Increase the levothyroxine dose by approximately 30% immediately — thyroid hormone requirements increase significantly in the first trimester; target TSH <2.5 mIU/L
options.C: Discontinue levothyroxine — thyroid hormone supplementation is contraindicated in pregnancy
options.D: Double the current dose to 176 mcg immediately — maternal hypothyroidism in the first trimester causes irreversible fetal neurological damage

questions.rationale: Pregnancy significantly increases levothyroxine requirements — typically by 30–50% starting in weeks 4–6 of the first trimester. Mechanism: increased TBG (thyroid-binding globulin) production from estrogen, increased placental type 3 deiodinase activity metabolizing T4, and fetal demand for maternal T4 (fetal thyroid not functional until weeks 10–12). TSH target in pregnancy: <2.5 mIU/L in first trimester, <3.0 mIU/L in second and third trimesters. The dose increase should occur IMMEDIATELY on confirmed pregnancy — not delayed to second trimester. A practical approach: add 2 additional levothyroxine doses per week (converts 7 doses/week to 9 doses/week = ~29% increase). Doubling the dose (option D) is excessive and risks iatrogenic hyperthyroidism. Discontinuing (option C) is never appropriate — untreated maternal hypothyroidism causes miscarriage and fetal neurodevelopmental impairment.

questions.stemAnchor: Stable hypothyroid + switching from brand Synthroid to generic — TSH monitoring after switch
questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with hypothyroidism has been stable on Synthroid (brand levothyroxine) 100 mcg daily for 5 years, with TSH consistently 1.5–2.0 mIU/L. Her insurance company has changed its formulary to require generic levothyroxine. She is switched to a generic manufacturer's product at the same dose. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: No monitoring is needed — FDA standards ensure all generics are bioequivalent
options.B: Check TSH in 6–8 weeks after the switch — bioavailability differences between manufacturers can cause clinically meaningful TSH changes
options.C: Immediately increase the generic dose by 25 mcg — generics always provide less thyroid hormone than brand
options.D: Refuse the generic substitution — generic levothyroxine should never be used in hypothyroid patients

questions.rationale: Levothyroxine has a narrow therapeutic index — small differences in bioavailability between manufacturers can result in clinically meaningful changes in TSH. While all generics meet FDA bioequivalence standards (within 80–125% of reference), the variation within that range can affect sensitive patients (particularly those with autoimmune thyroid disease or tight TSH targets). ATA/AACE recommendations: recheck TSH 6–8 weeks after any product change (brand to generic, generic to brand, or between generic manufacturers). This allows dose adjustment if needed before symptoms develop. Option A is incorrect — clinically meaningful TSH changes after switching have been documented. Option C (automatic dose increase) is inappropriate without data. Option D is overly restrictive — generics are acceptable with monitoring.

questions.stemAnchor: PPI use + hypothyroid + TSH rising without dose change — PPI as absorption inhibitor mechanism
questions.stem: A 50-year-old woman with primary hypothyroidism (stable TSH 2.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 112 mcg) was started on omeprazole 20 mg daily 4 months ago for GERD. Today's TSH is 5.8 mIU/L. She has not changed her levothyroxine dose, takes it at the same time (30 minutes before breakfast), and is not taking any other new medications. Which of the following best explains the TSH rise?

options.A: Omeprazole stimulates TSH secretion from the pituitary, unrelated to levothyroxine absorption
options.B: Omeprazole reduces gastric acid, which is necessary for the dissolution and absorption of levothyroxine tablets — this reduces levothyroxine bioavailability and requires dose adjustment or formulation change
options.C: Omeprazole is causing autoimmune thyroiditis that is worsening underlying Hashimoto disease
options.D: This TSH elevation is artifactual — PPIs interfere with TSH immunoassay results

questions.rationale: Proton pump inhibitors (PPIs) reduce gastric acid production. Levothyroxine tablets require an acidic gastric environment for optimal dissolution and absorption. With reduced gastric acid (from PPI use), levothyroxine absorption decreases, leading to lower serum T4 levels and compensatory TSH elevation. The TSH rise 4 months after PPI initiation is classic. Solutions: (1) Increase levothyroxine dose by 25 mcg and recheck TSH at 6–8 weeks, OR (2) Switch to a liquid levothyroxine formulation (Tirosint) which does not depend on gastric pH for absorption. PPIs do not affect TSH assays (option D) or cause autoimmune thyroiditis (option C). This is a high-yield absorption interaction in SF-02 slugB scope.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G3b + UACR 380 + BP 148/88 + on ACEi — SGLT-2 inhibitor add-on for CKD progression CREDENCE/DAPA-CKD
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with CKD (eGFR 38 mL/min, UACR 380 mg/g) and T2DM is on lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 500 mg twice daily. His BP is 148/88 mmHg and A1c is 7.8%. Despite lisinopril, his UACR has not significantly decreased over 6 months. Which of the following additional medication is most supported by current evidence to further slow CKD progression?

options.A: Add an ARB to the lisinopril for dual RAAS blockade to further reduce proteinuria
options.B: Add an SGLT-2 inhibitor (canagliflozin or dapagliflozin) — CREDENCE and DAPA-CKD trials demonstrated CKD progression reduction with SGLT-2 inhibitors in T2DM + CKD with proteinuria
options.C: Add spironolactone to reduce proteinuria — aldosterone antagonists have proven renal benefit in CKD
options.D: Increase lisinopril to 40 mg and recheck in 3 months

questions.rationale: SGLT-2 inhibitors have robust evidence for CKD progression reduction in patients with T2DM and CKD with proteinuria: CREDENCE trial (canagliflozin, eGFR 30–90, UACR >300) and DAPA-CKD trial (dapagliflozin, eGFR 25–75, UACR ≥200 — REGARDLESS of diabetes status) both showed significant reduction in CKD progression events. Current guideline recommendation: add SGLT-2 inhibitor when UACR ≥200 mg/g and eGFR ≥20–30. This patient (UACR 380, eGFR 38) clearly meets criteria. Option A (dual RAAS blockade ACEi + ARB) is contraindicated — ONTARGET trial showed increased AKI and hyperkalemia without added benefit. Spironolactone (option C) adds hyperkalemia risk in CKD without the proven CKD progression benefit seen with SGLT-2 inhibitors. Increasing lisinopril to maximum dose alone (option D) is reasonable but less impactful than adding SGLT-2i.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G4 + eGFR 22 + rapidly declining + no referral yet — nephrology referral criteria
questions.stem: A 66-year-old woman with CKD has had the following eGFR trajectory: 42 (18 months ago), 34 (12 months ago), 26 (6 months ago), 22 today. She has UACR 620 mg/g and BP 134/82 on lisinopril 40 mg and amlodipine 10 mg. She has never been referred to nephrology. Which of the following correctly identifies the referral indication and most appropriate action?

options.A: Nephrology referral is only required at eGFR <15 or when dialysis is imminent
options.B: Refer to nephrology urgently — eGFR <30 (G4), rapid GFR decline (>5 mL/min/year), and macroalbuminuria all meet referral criteria that should have occurred earlier
options.C: Continue current management — eGFR 22 is G4 and can be managed in primary care until dialysis is needed
options.D: Add furosemide for fluid management and recheck eGFR in 6 months before deciding on referral

questions.rationale: KDIGO and ADA CKD nephrology referral criteria include: (1) eGFR <30 mL/min (G4 or worse), (2) rapidly declining eGFR >5 mL/min/year — this patient has declined from 42 to 22 over 18 months = ~13 mL/min/year decline, well above threshold, (3) nephrotic-range proteinuria or UACR ≥300 persistently, (4) refractory hyperkalemia, (5) uncontrolled hypertension despite ≥3 agents. This patient meets at least THREE referral criteria simultaneously and has NEVER seen nephrology — this is a significant care gap. Early nephrology involvement: optimizes CKD-MBD management, prepares for renal replacement therapy (modality education, fistula creation planning for HD, PD training), and manages complex complications. Option A sets an inappropriately late referral threshold.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G3a + hyperkalemia K 5.7 on ACEi + spironolactone — management hyperkalemia without losing renoprotection
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with CKD (eGFR 44 mL/min, UACR 210 mg/g), HFrEF (EF 35%), and hypertension is on lisinopril 20 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, carvedilol 12.5 mg BID, and furosemide 40 mg. Today's BMP: Na 140, K 5.7, CO2 21, Cr 1.5. BP 128/78. He has no symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate management of his hyperkalemia?

options.A: Hold both lisinopril AND spironolactone immediately — K 5.7 requires removal of all potassium-retaining medications
options.B: Hold spironolactone (highest K risk in this combination), assess dietary potassium intake, consider sodium bicarbonate for metabolic acidosis (CO2 21), and optimize furosemide dosing; recheck BMP in 1–2 weeks
options.C: Increase furosemide to 80 mg daily only — diuresis will lower the potassium without removing renally protective medications
options.D: Add patiromer (potassium binder) immediately to continue all four GDMT medications unchanged

questions.rationale: K+ 5.7 is moderate hyperkalemia — not immediately life-threatening but requires action. In CKD + HFrEF, the goal is to address hyperkalemia while preserving renoprotective and cardioprotective therapy as much as possible. Hierarchy of interventions: (1) Hold the most dispensable potassium-retaining agent: spironolactone (MRA adds the least unique organ protection when ACEi is already present, vs lisinopril which is critical for both HF and CKD); (2) Address metabolic acidosis (CO2 21 = bicarbonate 21, below 22 threshold) with sodium bicarbonate — acidosis shifts K+ extracellularly; (3) Dietary potassium restriction counseling; (4) Optimize furosemide to improve tubular K+ excretion. Recheck BMP in 1–2 weeks. Patiromer (option D) can be used but is not the first step — dietary and medication optimization should precede potassium binder introduction. Holding both lisinopril and spironolactone (option A) is overly aggressive for K+ 5.7 without symptoms.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD + dietary sodium 3.8g per day + persistent HTN + proteinuria — dietary sodium reduction renoprotective
questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman with CKD G3a (eGFR 48 mL/min, UACR 320 mg/g) and hypertension (BP 144/90 on lisinopril 20 mg + amlodipine 10 mg) is struggling with BP control. A 24-hour dietary recall estimates her sodium intake at approximately 3,800 mg/day. She denies NSAID use or medication non-adherence. Which of the following intervention is most appropriate as the next step before adding a third antihypertensive?

options.A: Dietary sodium restriction to <2g/day — reducing sodium intake reduces BP and independently reduces proteinuria in CKD
options.B: Sodium restriction is not recommended in CKD — patients need adequate sodium for blood volume maintenance
options.C: Add spironolactone as the third agent without addressing dietary factors
options.D: Switch from amlodipine to chlorthalidone — thiazide diuretics increase sodium excretion and are more effective

questions.rationale: Dietary sodium restriction (<2g = <5g NaCl/day) is one of the most important non-pharmacological interventions for CKD: (1) reduces BP through volume reduction, (2) DIRECTLY reduces proteinuria (high sodium diet stimulates RAAS → glomerular hyperfiltration → increased proteinuria), (3) enhances the efficacy of RAAS inhibitors (high sodium intake blunts the antiproteinuric effect of ACEi/ARBs). This patient is consuming nearly double the recommended amount (3,800 mg vs <2,000 mg target). Sodium reduction should be the next intervention before adding a third antihypertensive — it may provide the additional BP/proteinuria reduction needed. Option B is incorrect — sodium restriction is safe and recommended in CKD; it is potassium restriction that is sometimes needed in advanced CKD. Chlorthalidone (option D) is a reasonable add-on but addressing the modifiable dietary factor first is more cost-effective.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G3b + metabolic acidosis bicarb 18 — sodium bicarbonate indication and CKD progression
questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with CKD G3b (eGFR 36 mL/min) has the following BMP: Na 140, K 4.6, Cl 108, CO2 18, BUN 28, Cr 1.9. He has no dyspnea and is not hyperventilating. His diet is unrestricted. Which of the following best represents the appropriate management of his metabolic findings?

options.A: No treatment needed — bicarbonate of 18 is a lab value and patients only require treatment for symptoms
options.B: Initiate sodium bicarbonate supplementation — serum bicarbonate <22 mEq/L in CKD is associated with accelerated CKD progression; the BICARBONATE study supports treatment to maintain bicarbonate ≥22 mEq/L
options.C: Initiate IV sodium bicarbonate for acute metabolic acidosis management
options.D: Start acetazolamide to improve renal acid excretion

questions.rationale: Metabolic acidosis in CKD (bicarbonate <22 mEq/L) is a nephrotoxic complication: (1) activates complement and tubular injury pathways that directly accelerate CKD progression, (2) increases protein catabolism → uremia, (3) impairs bone buffering → worsens renal osteodystrophy. Multiple observational studies and the BICARBONATE trial support: treating CKD acidosis with oral sodium bicarbonate (650 mg to 1.3g two to three times daily) to maintain serum bicarbonate ≥22 mEq/L slows eGFR decline. This is now recommended in KDIGO 2024 guidelines. Option A (treat only if symptomatic) misses the opportunity to slow CKD progression. IV bicarbonate (option C) is for severe acute acidosis (pH <7.1) — not for chronic CKD acidosis management. Acetazolamide (option D) WORSENS metabolic acidosis — it is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that reduces bicarbonate reabsorption.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G4 + smoking 25 pack-year + eGFR declining — smoking cessation as CKD modifier
questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with CKD G4 (eGFR 24 mL/min) and T2DM has a 30-pack-year smoking history and currently smokes 1 ppd. His BP is well-controlled on 3 antihypertensives. His nephrologist's note states his eGFR has declined more rapidly than expected for his comorbidities and questions modifiable factors. Which of the following intervention should be identified and prioritized at this visit?

options.A: Add a fourth antihypertensive — BP control is the only modifiable factor
options.B: Initiate smoking cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy — smoking independently accelerates CKD progression by 40–60% and is the most important unaddressed modifiable factor
options.C: Smoking cessation has no proven benefit in CKD — focus only on BP control and glycemic management
options.D: Refer to physical therapy for exercise training — exercise alone can reverse CKD progression

questions.rationale: Smoking accelerates CKD progression through multiple mechanisms: (1) renal vasoconstriction reducing GFR, (2) endothelial dysfunction and glomerular hyperfiltration, (3) worsening of diabetic nephropathy and hypertensive nephrosclerosis, (4) increased oxidative stress and inflammation. Studies demonstrate that smokers with CKD have approximately 40–60% faster eGFR decline compared to non-smokers. Smoking cessation slows this excess decline. In a patient already on optimal medical therapy with unexplained rapid decline, smoking is the key unaddressed modifiable risk factor. Counseling + pharmacotherapy (varenicline first-line for most patients; bupropion if varenicline not tolerated; NRT) should be initiated. Option C is incorrect — smoking cessation is strongly supported as a CKD modifier. Exercise (option D) improves CV health and is beneficial but does not directly reverse CKD progression.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G4 + Hgb 10.1 + TSAT 16% + ferritin 85 — anemia of CKD iron-first approach
questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with CKD G4 (eGFR 22 mL/min) has a hemoglobin of 10.1 g/dL. Iron studies show: serum iron 54 mcg/dL, TIBC 280, ferritin 85 ng/mL, transferrin saturation (TSAT) 16%. She is not on dialysis. She is fatigued but does not require transfusion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Initiate erythropoiesis-stimulating agent (ESA) immediately — anemia of CKD is from EPO deficiency
options.B: Treat iron deficiency first — TSAT <20% and ferritin <200 ng/mL indicate iron-deficient erythropoiesis; oral iron is first-line but IV iron preferred in G4 due to poor GI absorption
options.C: Transfuse 2 units of packed red blood cells for symptomatic anemia
options.D: Order reticulocyte count and B12/folate before treating — anemia of CKD must be a diagnosis of exclusion

questions.rationale: Anemia management in CKD: IRON FIRST before ESA. TSAT <20% OR ferritin <200 ng/mL (in non-dialysis CKD) indicates iron-deficient erythropoiesis — iron supplementation should be given before or alongside ESA. If ESA is started without adequate iron stores, it will not stimulate effective erythropoiesis. In G4 CKD (eGFR 22): oral iron has poor GI absorption and side effects; IV iron (ferric gluconate, iron sucrose, ferumoxytol) is preferred for faster and more reliable iron repletion. After iron optimization (TSAT ≥20%, ferritin ≥200 ng/mL): if Hgb remains <10 g/dL, consider ESA referral through nephrology. Transfusion (option C) sensitizes patients to HLA antigens (affects transplant candidacy) and should be avoided unless symptomatic anemia with Hgb <8 or hemodynamic compromise. Reticulocyte count (option D) is part of the workup but is not needed before treating obvious iron deficiency.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD + PTH 280 + phosphate 5.8 + calcium normal — secondary hyperparathyroidism management sequence
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with CKD G4 (eGFR 24 mL/min) has the following labs at his quarterly visit: calcium 9.1 mg/dL (normal), phosphate 5.8 mg/dL (elevated), PTH 280 pg/mL (elevated), 25-OH Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient). He is not on any mineral metabolism medications. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial management of his CKD-MBD?

options.A: Start calcitriol (active Vitamin D) immediately to suppress PTH — active Vitamin D is first-line for elevated PTH in CKD
options.B: Address phosphate and Vitamin D deficiency first: dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binder + Vitamin D3 repletion; recheck labs before adding active Vitamin D
options.C: Start cinacalcet (calcimimetic) to lower PTH — most potent PTH suppressor available
options.D: Refer immediately for parathyroidectomy — PTH 280 requires surgical management

questions.rationale: CKD-mineral bone disease (CKD-MBD) management sequence: (1) Control hyperphosphatemia FIRST — dietary phosphate restriction + phosphate binder (calcium carbonate or sevelamer); (2) Correct Vitamin D deficiency (25-OH D 18 = deficient) with Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) — this partially suppresses PTH by improving calcium absorption; (3) Reassess PTH after phosphate control and Vitamin D repletion; (4) If PTH remains elevated despite optimization, THEN add calcitriol (active 1,25 Vit D) for secondary hyperparathyroidism; (5) Cinacalcet is used when calcitriol is insufficient or hypercalcemia prevents calcitriol use. Skipping steps 1–2 and immediately using calcitriol (option A) or cinacalcet (option C) adds complexity without addressing underlying hyperphosphatemia. PTH 280 is elevated but manageable with conservative measures — parathyroidectomy (option D) is reserved for refractory tertiary hyperparathyroidism (usually in dialysis patients with PTH >800 pg/mL).

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G4 + K 6.2 + ECG peaked T waves — hyperkalemia emergency management sequence
questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with CKD G4 (eGFR 19 mL/min) and HFrEF presents with fatigue and muscle weakness. He is on lisinopril, carvedilol, furosemide 80 mg, and spironolactone 25 mg. BMP: K+ 6.2 mEq/L. ECG shows peaked T waves in V2-V4. BP 136/84. He is alert. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management sequence?

options.A: Increase furosemide to 160 mg to rapidly excrete potassium
options.B: Call 911 — peaked T waves indicate cardiac toxicity; transfer to ED for IV calcium gluconate, insulin + dextrose, and sodium bicarbonate, with emergent nephrology
options.C: Administer oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate) and recheck K+ in 6 hours
options.D: Reduce lisinopril dose and hold spironolactone; repeat BMP tomorrow

questions.rationale: K+ 6.2 WITH ECG changes (peaked T waves = earliest cardiac manifestation of hyperkalemia) is a medical emergency requiring immediate transfer. Peaked T waves signal the myocardium is already affected — the next ECG progression is PR prolongation → wide QRS → sine wave → VF → asystole. Emergency management sequence in the ED: (1) IV calcium gluconate 10 mL 10% solution over 10 minutes — membrane stabilization (buys time, does NOT lower K+ level); (2) Insulin 10 units IV + dextrose 50% — shifts K+ into cells (onset 15–30 min); (3) Sodium bicarbonate — shifts K+ into cells, especially with concurrent acidosis; (4) Albuterol nebulized — shifts K+ into cells; (5) Definitive removal: furosemide IV, kayexalate, or dialysis (preferred in dialysis-dependent or severe refractory hyperkalemia). Oral kayexalate (option C) in an emergency with ECG changes is dangerously slow — onset 2–4 hours. Holding medications alone (option D) is inappropriate for K+ 6.2 with ECG changes.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD + protein restriction 0.8g/kg vs higher protein diet debate — KDIGO recommendation
questions.stem: A 57-year-old man with CKD G3b (eGFR 36 mL/min) asks about dietary protein intake. He currently consumes approximately 1.4 g/kg/day of protein (primarily red meat). His nephrologist has mentioned protein restriction. Which of the following most accurately reflects current KDIGO guideline recommendations on protein intake in CKD?

options.A: High protein intake (>1.3 g/kg/day) has no effect on CKD progression — diet is irrelevant
options.B: Moderate protein restriction (0.6–0.8 g/kg/day) is recommended in CKD G3b–G5 not on dialysis — reduces urea generation, glomerular hyperfiltration, and may slow CKD progression
options.C: All patients with CKD should consume >1.2 g/kg/day to prevent malnutrition
options.D: Protein restriction below 0.3 g/kg/day is required in all stages of CKD

questions.rationale: KDIGO 2024 CKD guidelines recommend: for CKD G3b–G5 not on dialysis, moderate protein intake of 0.6–0.8 g/kg/day (versus the population average of ~1.2 g/kg/day). Mechanism: high protein intake increases glomerular filtration and intraglomerular pressure, worsening proteinuria and accelerating CKD progression. This patient consumes 1.4 g/kg/day — significantly above the recommended range. Additional guidance: high-quality protein sources (plant-based preferred) reduce urea load less than animal protein. Important caveat: excessive protein restriction (<0.6 g/kg/day) risks malnutrition in already catabolic CKD patients — this is why the recommendation is 0.6–0.8, not lower. On dialysis, requirements are HIGHER (1.0–1.2 g/kg/day) due to dialysate amino acid losses.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD + vitamin D3 deficiency vs calcitriol — when to use each in CKD stages
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with CKD G3a (eGFR 48 mL/min) has a 25-OH Vitamin D level of 16 ng/mL (deficient). PTH is 82 pg/mL (mildly elevated). Calcium and phosphorus are normal. Which of the following correctly describes the appropriate Vitamin D supplementation strategy?

options.A: Start calcitriol (1,25-OH Vitamin D) 0.25 mcg daily — active Vitamin D is always the correct form in CKD
options.B: Supplement with cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2000–4000 IU daily to correct 25-OH D deficiency — reserve calcitriol for more advanced CKD (G4–G5) with significant secondary hyperparathyroidism
options.C: No supplementation needed — Vitamin D supplementation is only indicated in dialysis patients
options.D: Calcitriol is contraindicated in CKD because it causes hypercalcemia in all patients

questions.rationale: In CKD G3, the kidney still converts 25-OH D to calcitriol (1,25-OH D) — deficiency in 25-OH D is common and should be corrected with Vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) or Vitamin D2. In early to moderate CKD, the primary issue is substrate deficiency — insufficient 25-OH D available for renal 1-alpha-hydroxylation. Calcitriol (active Vitamin D) is reserved for G4–G5 or when significant secondary hyperparathyroidism persists despite adequate 25-OH D levels and phosphate control — because by G4–G5, the kidney can no longer activate 25-OH D effectively. Using calcitriol prematurely in G3 without hyperphosphatemia can cause hypercalcemia and suppress PTH to dangerously low levels (adynamic bone disease). This patient (G3a, mild PTH elevation, normal calcium/phosphorus) should receive cholecalciferol to correct substrate deficiency — not calcitriol.

questions.stemAnchor: CKD G4 + uremic symptoms — fatigue nausea decreased appetite + target for RRT discussion
questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman with CKD G4 (eGFR 18 mL/min) presents with worsening fatigue, decreased appetite, nausea, and difficulty concentrating over 2 months. Her nephrologist has mentioned she is 'approaching the need for dialysis.' Which of the following is the most appropriate NP action at this visit?

options.A: Increase diuretic therapy — uremic symptoms are caused by fluid overload
options.B: Discuss renal replacement therapy (RRT) modalities, confirm nephrology is engaged, and ensure timely arteriovenous (AV) fistula creation referral if hemodialysis is the selected modality
options.C: Begin ESA therapy to treat the fatigue — erythropoietin will resolve all uremic symptoms
options.D: Order CT abdomen to evaluate for other causes of nausea before attributing to CKD

questions.rationale: Uremia symptoms (fatigue, anorexia, nausea, cognitive changes) at G4/G5 indicate the patient is approaching the need for renal replacement therapy (RRT). Key NP role at this stage: (1) Ensure nephrology is actively co-managing the patient, (2) Discuss RRT modality options: hemodialysis (HD — requires AV fistula or graft creation at least 6 months before RRT initiation), peritoneal dialysis (PD — catheter placement), kidney transplant (listing), or conservative management; (3) Facilitate timely referral to vascular surgery for AV fistula creation if HD is selected — fistulas require 3–6 months to mature and are preferred over tunneled catheters. Delaying fistula creation until the patient is on dialysis forces catheter use with higher infection risk. ESA (option C) may address anemia but does not correct uremia. Uremia symptoms with eGFR 18 are expected — CT scan for other causes is low yield in this context.

questions.stemAnchor: 62-year-old smoker + FEV1 52% + CAT 15 + 1 exacerbation year — GOLD 2B inhaler therapy
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with progressive dyspnea on exertion. Spirometry shows: FEV1/FVC 0.58 (post-bronchodilator), FEV1 52% predicted. He has 1 moderate COPD exacerbation in the past year (treated with prednisone + antibiotic outpatient). CAT score 15, mMRC 2. He is on SABA (albuterol) PRN only. Which of the following GOLD classification and most appropriate pharmacotherapy represents the current standard?

options.A: GOLD 2, Group A — continue SABA PRN only; add LAMA if symptoms worsen
options.B: GOLD 2, Group B — initiate LAMA (tiotropium) or LABA as the first-line maintenance bronchodilator; consider LAMA + LABA if symptoms persist
options.C: GOLD 2, Group E — initiate triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) for high exacerbation risk
options.D: GOLD 3, Group E — initiate ICS alone (fluticasone) to reduce exacerbation frequency

questions.rationale: GOLD classification: FEV1 52% predicted = GOLD 2 (50–79%). Exacerbation risk: 1 moderate exacerbation (outpatient treatment = moderate, not hospitalized) = Group B (1 moderate exacerbation, not ≥2 moderate or ≥1 leading to hospitalization which would be Group E). CAT 15 and mMRC 2 confirm significant symptom burden. Group B management: start LAMA (tiotropium, umeclidinium, glycopyrrolate) OR LABA as maintenance monotherapy; if persistent symptoms → LAMA + LABA dual bronchodilation. ICS is NOT indicated for Group B without eosinophilia or ≥2 exacerbations. Group E (option C) requires ≥2 moderate exacerbations OR ≥1 hospitalization — this patient had only 1 moderate exacerbation. ICS monotherapy (option D) is NEVER used in COPD — it is only used as part of triple therapy in Group E. GOLD 3 requires FEV1 <50%.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + eosinophil count 380 + 3 exacerbations last year on LAMA + LABA — ICS addition for high eosinophil and exacerbation history
questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with COPD has had 3 moderate exacerbations in the past year despite being on tiotropium (LAMA) + formoterol (LABA). Blood eosinophil count is 380 cells/μL. FEV1 38% predicted (GOLD 3). CAT score 22. Which of the following represents the most appropriate next step?

options.A: Add ICS (fluticasone) — eosinophil count ≥300 cells/μL + ≥2 exacerbations on LAMA+LABA is a strong indication for triple therapy
options.B: Switch to SABA + SAMA only — simplify the regimen as it is not working
options.C: Add azithromycin 250 mg daily without ICS — antibiotics are more effective than ICS in preventing exacerbations
options.D: Refer to pulmonology for lung volume reduction surgery (LVRS)

questions.rationale: Triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) is indicated in COPD Group E (≥2 exacerbations/year or ≥1 hospitalization) when eosinophil count ≥300 cells/μL — the TRIBUTE and IMPACT trials showed triple therapy reduced exacerbations more than dual bronchodilation in this subgroup. This patient: GOLD 3, Group E (3 exacerbations), eosinophil 380 (≥300) — meets both criteria for ICS addition. Key caveat: ICS in COPD carries a ~3% absolute increased risk of pneumonia vs LAMA+LABA — this risk-benefit must be weighed. The GOLD 2023 recommendation: ICS should be considered when eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL OR when eosinophils 100–300 + ≥2 exacerbations. Azithromycin prophylaxis (option C) is an evidence-based add-on for frequent exacerbators (AZALEA, Albert trials) but comes after triple therapy is maximized. LVRS (option D) is for severe emphysema with specific spirometric criteria — not the next step here.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD patient + inhaler technique poor — device-specific education before stepping up therapy
questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with COPD (GOLD 2, Group B) has been on tiotropium HandiHaler (dry powder inhaler) for 6 months with poor symptomatic control (CAT score 18, mMRC 2). You observe him using his inhaler in clinic and note significant errors: he does not breathe in forcefully through the device, tilts his head forward, and fails to hold his breath after inhalation. He does not use a spacer. Which of the following represents the most appropriate initial response before changing or adding medications?

options.A: Add a LABA (salmeterol) to the tiotropium immediately — poor symptom control indicates LAMA alone is insufficient
options.B: Switch to a soft mist inhaler (Respimat) or reassess inhaler technique with education and re-demonstration — correct technique before escalating therapy
options.C: Add an ICS (fluticasone) for additional anti-inflammatory effect
options.D: Refer to pulmonary rehabilitation — poor inhaler technique is irrelevant to outcomes

questions.rationale: Incorrect inhaler technique is extremely common (studies show >75% of COPD patients use inhalers incorrectly) and is a major cause of apparent treatment failure. Before stepping up therapy, the NP must: (1) Assess technique at each visit, (2) Correct errors with demonstration and repeat demonstration (teach-back), (3) Consider switching to a device better suited to the patient's ability — soft mist inhalers (Respimat) require less inspiratory effort than dry powder inhalers (DPIs) and may be better tolerated in elderly or low peak inspiratory flow patients. Adding a second drug (options A and C) before confirming adequate drug delivery from the first device will compound the problem and incur additional cost. Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise tolerance but does not address inhaler delivery.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + home oxygen indication — SpO2 88% at rest on room air eGFR lung criteria
questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with COPD (GOLD 4, Group E) presents for a follow-up visit. SpO2 at rest on room air is 87%. Arterial blood gas shows PaO2 54 mmHg, PaCO2 52 mmHg. She walks slowly with moderate dyspnea at rest. FEV1 is 28% predicted. Which of the following represents the most appropriate intervention for her supplemental oxygen status?

options.A: Do not prescribe supplemental oxygen — the MRC study only showed benefit in continuous use, which patients rarely adhere to
options.B: Prescribe supplemental oxygen for nighttime use only — daytime oxygen is not beneficial
options.C: Prescribe long-term supplemental oxygen (LTOT) for continuous use ≥15 hours/day — resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg (SpO2 ≤88%) meets the Medicare/guideline criteria; LTOT reduces pulmonary hypertension and improves survival
options.D: Prescribe oxygen only during exercise — resting hypoxemia does not benefit from supplemental oxygen

questions.rationale: Long-term supplemental oxygen therapy (LTOT) indications: resting PaO2 ≤55 mmHg OR SpO2 ≤88% on room air — this patient meets both (PaO2 54, SpO2 87%). LTOT indications: (1) PaO2 ≤55 mmHg or SpO2 ≤88%; (2) PaO2 56–59 mmHg with cor pulmonale, polycythemia (Hct >55%), or significant desaturation during sleep. Benefits of LTOT (≥15 hours/day): (1) reduces progression of pulmonary hypertension, (2) reduces right heart failure (cor pulmonale), (3) improves survival in severe COPD (MRC trial, NOTT trial). Nighttime-only oxygen (option B) is insufficient — at least 15 hours/day is required. Option D is incorrect — exercise-only oxygen has a different indication criterion (desaturation on exertion).

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + roflumilast indication — chronic bronchitis + FEV1 <50% + frequent exacerbator Group E
questions.stem: A 65-year-old male smoker (former) with COPD (FEV1 38%, Group E — 3 exacerbations last year) is on triple therapy (LAMA + LABA + ICS) and still having frequent exacerbations. He has a chronic productive cough with daily mucus production (chronic bronchitis). Which of the following additional pharmacological intervention is most appropriate?

options.A: Add roflumilast 500 mcg daily — a PDE4 inhibitor indicated for COPD with chronic bronchitis + FEV1 <50% + frequent exacerbations on triple therapy
options.B: Add montelukast — leukotriene receptor antagonists are approved for COPD exacerbation prevention
options.C: Add inhaled N-acetylcysteine — mucolytic therapy reduces exacerbation frequency in all COPD patients
options.D: Add a long-acting muscarinic antagonist (LAMA) — adding a second LAMA increases bronchodilation

questions.rationale: Roflumilast (Daliresp) is a phosphodiesterase-4 (PDE4) inhibitor approved specifically for: COPD with chronic bronchitis (daily productive cough), FEV1 <50% predicted, and ≥2 exacerbations per year despite maximal bronchodilator therapy. It reduces exacerbation frequency by approximately 15–20% in this specific subgroup. It is an ORAL anti-inflammatory agent (not a bronchodilator). Main adverse effects: nausea, diarrhea, weight loss (significant in some patients — monitor weight monthly), psychiatric effects (depression, suicidal ideation — boxed warning). Contraindicated in patients with depression or BMI <18.5. Montelukast (option B) is not approved for COPD. N-acetylcysteine (option C) has limited evidence in COPD. Adding a second LAMA (option D) is not indicated — this patient already has LAMA in triple therapy.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + smoking cessation + varenicline option — most effective intervention for COPD progression
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with newly diagnosed COPD (GOLD 1, Group A) currently smokes 1 ppd (35 pack-years). He is started on albuterol PRN. He asks: 'Is there anything I can do to stop my lungs from getting worse?' Which of the following single intervention has the most evidence for slowing COPD progression in this patient?

options.A: Start tiotropium LAMA — daily maintenance bronchodilator prevents lung function decline
options.B: Smoking cessation — the only intervention proven to reduce the annual rate of FEV1 decline in COPD; varenicline is the most effective pharmacotherapy
options.C: Pulmonary rehabilitation — exercise training prevents emphysema progression
options.D: Supplemental oxygen during sleep — nighttime hypoxemia drives COPD progression

questions.rationale: Smoking cessation is the single most effective intervention to slow COPD progression. Evidence: the Lung Health Study demonstrated that sustained smoking cessation reduces annual FEV1 decline from approximately 50 mL/year (continuing smokers) to 25 mL/year (quitters = normal aging rate). No COPD medication (including bronchodilators, ICS, or PDE4 inhibitors) has demonstrated the same magnitude of disease modification as smoking cessation. Pharmacotherapy for cessation: varenicline (Chantix) has the highest 12-month abstinence rates (approximately 22–26%) compared to bupropion (18%) and NRT (15%). COPD Group A management (FEV1 ≥80%, low symptoms, no exacerbations) focuses primarily on smoking cessation + SABA PRN — not yet initiating maintenance bronchodilators unless symptomatic.

questions.stemAnchor: ACO patient — asthma features + COPD features — management approach for asthma-COPD overlap
questions.stem: A 54-year-old woman has both significant smoking history (30 pack-years) and a childhood history of asthma. Spirometry shows: FEV1/FVC 0.65 (below 0.7), FEV1 64%, with a bronchodilator response of 320 mL and 15% improvement after SABA (meets asthma reversibility criteria). She has frequent nocturnal symptoms, elevated blood eosinophils (520 cells/μL), and a FENO (fractional exhaled nitric oxide) of 38 ppb. Which of the following management approach is most appropriate for asthma-COPD overlap (ACO)?

options.A: Treat as pure COPD: start LAMA + LABA, avoid ICS
options.B: Treat as pure asthma: start ICS + LABA only, avoid LAMA
options.C: Treat as ACO: ICS + LABA is the foundational therapy (given asthma features); a LAMA may be added for persistent airflow obstruction; specialist referral recommended
options.D: Recommend SABA-only therapy until the diagnosis of asthma vs COPD is definitively confirmed

questions.rationale: Asthma-COPD Overlap (ACO): features of BOTH asthma (significant bronchodilator reversibility ≥12% and ≥200 mL, eosinophilia, elevated FENO, nocturnal symptoms, atopic features) AND COPD (smoking history >10 pack-years, persistent airflow obstruction post-bronchodilator). Management principle: ICS is essential in ACO because of the asthmatic features (eosinophilic airway inflammation) — avoiding ICS (as in GOLD pure COPD option A) risks severe asthma exacerbations. LABA is added to ICS for bronchodilation. LAMA may be added for persistent obstruction. Specialist referral is recommended because ACO management is complex and inhaler titration decisions require further evaluation. SABA-only (option D) is inadequate for established ACO with persistent symptoms.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD moderate exacerbation + purulent sputum + antibiotic selection + 5-day steroids — Anthonisen criteria
questions.stem: A 66-year-old man with COPD (GOLD 2, Group E on LAMA + LABA) presents with a 3-day history of increased dyspnea, increased sputum production that has become yellow-green, and worsening cough. He has no fever. SpO2 89% on room air (usual 92%). Chest auscultation reveals mild expiratory wheezing bilaterally. He has no pneumonia on CXR. He can manage at home with support. Which of the following represents the most appropriate outpatient exacerbation management?

options.A: Albuterol PRN only — mild exacerbations do not require antibiotics or corticosteroids
options.B: Prednisone 40 mg/day × 5 days + amoxicillin-clavulanate OR azithromycin + increase short-acting bronchodilator use; daily check-in by phone
options.C: Doxycycline 100 mg twice daily alone — antibiotics without steroids are adequate for COPD exacerbations
options.D: IV methylprednisolone 125 mg every 6 hours — all exacerbations require high-dose IV steroids

questions.rationale: This COPD exacerbation meets 3 Anthonisen criteria (all three): increased dyspnea + increased sputum volume + purulent sputum = Type 1 exacerbation — antibiotics AND systemic steroids are indicated. Management: (1) Oral prednisone 40 mg daily × 5 days (REDUCE trial: 5-day course equivalent to 14-day, less side effects), (2) Antibiotic: uncomplicated exacerbation → amoxicillin-clavulanate or doxycycline or azithromycin; risk factors (FEV1 <50%, ≥2 exac/year, prior Pseudomonas) → ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin, (3) Increase SABA frequency (albuterol every 4–6 hours), (4) Phone monitoring at 24–48 hours. IV methylprednisolone (option D) is for inpatient management or inability to take oral therapy. This patient does not meet hospitalization criteria (no hemodynamic instability, no severe respiratory failure, manageable at home).

questions.stemAnchor: COPD exacerbation hospitalization criteria — pH 7.28 pCO2 58 + confusion + hypoxemia
questions.stem: A 74-year-old woman with COPD (GOLD 3, Group E) presents with worsening dyspnea over 24 hours. She appears confused and is using accessory muscles. Vitals: SpO2 82% on room air, RR 28, HR 112, BP 104/68. ABG: pH 7.28, PaO2 48 mmHg, PaCO2 58 mmHg. She has been on her nebulizers every 2 hours at home without improvement. Which of the following best represents the most appropriate disposition?

options.A: Start oral prednisone 40 mg and azithromycin; have her return in 24 hours
options.B: Give albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers in clinic and observe for 2 hours before deciding on admission
options.C: Call 911 for immediate hospital transfer — this exacerbation meets criteria for hospitalization: acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.28), SpO2 82%, confusion, hemodynamic instability
options.D: Start BiPAP at home via a rented machine — non-invasive ventilation avoids hospitalization

questions.rationale: COPD exacerbation hospitalization criteria (any one of): severe dyspnea with inadequate response to initial therapy, altered mental status (present — confusion), hypoxemia (SpO2 <88% or PaO2 <55 mmHg — present), acute hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidosis (pH <7.35 and pCO2 >45 — present: pH 7.28), hemodynamic instability, or inability to manage at home. This patient meets multiple criteria simultaneously. She requires: (1) Hospital admission, (2) Controlled oxygen to SpO2 88–92% (avoid supranormal O2 in CO2 retainers), (3) NIV/BiPAP for acute hypercapnic RF (pH 7.25–7.35 is the range with strongest BiPAP evidence — this patient at 7.28 is in the ideal range), (4) IV methylprednisolone, (5) IV/nebulized bronchodilators. Home BiPAP (option D) is not standard of care and is unsafe without monitoring in a confused patient with hemodynamic instability.

questions.stemAnchor: Post-COPD hospitalization — 30-day readmission prevention follow-up within 30 days
questions.stem: A 69-year-old woman was discharged from the hospital 8 days ago after a severe COPD exacerbation (her second hospitalization in 12 months). She is now on triple therapy (umeclidinium/vilanterol/fluticasone inhaler), tiotropium was changed to the combination, and she received a 10-day prednisone taper. She presents for a post-discharge follow-up. Which of the following interventions is most critical for reducing her 30-day COPD readmission risk?

options.A: Increase her steroid dose — she is at high risk of another exacerbation
options.B: Review inhaler technique, medication adherence, and action plan; refer to pulmonary rehabilitation; smoking cessation if applicable; schedule follow-up within 4 weeks
options.C: Hospitalize for observation — patients with two hospitalizations in 12 months should be admitted prophylactically
options.D: Switch to home BiPAP — all COPD patients after hospitalization benefit from nocturnal non-invasive ventilation

questions.rationale: Post-COPD hospitalization care bundle to reduce 30-day readmission: (1) Inhaler technique review and correction — the most commonly missed step; (2) Confirm understanding of and adherence to new medications; (3) Provide a written COPD action plan (when to call the provider, when to go to ED); (4) Pulmonary rehabilitation referral — REACT trial showed early pulmonary rehabilitation after hospitalization reduces readmissions; (5) Smoking cessation if applicable; (6) Follow-up within 4 weeks of discharge (early follow-up significantly reduces 30-day readmission); (7) Influenza + pneumococcal vaccination status check. 30-day COPD readmission rates are a quality measure — this bundle of interventions is evidence-based. Prophylactic hospitalization (option C) is not a standard practice.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + cor pulmonale + pedal edema + JVP elevation — supplemental oxygen as treatment, not diuresis alone
questions.stem: A 74-year-old man with severe COPD (GOLD 4, Group E) presents with progressive bilateral lower extremity edema over 3 weeks. JVD is present. HR 96, BP 114/72, SpO2 86% on room air. ECG shows right axis deviation and P pulmonale. BNP is 182 pg/mL. Echocardiogram shows RV dilation and estimated RVSP 58 mmHg. Which of the following best represents the primary treatment target for his cor pulmonale?

options.A: Start furosemide 40 mg daily as the primary treatment — volume overload is the primary problem
options.B: Supplemental oxygen (LTOT) is the primary treatment — chronic hypoxemia drives hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction; oxygen reduces pulmonary arterial pressures and improves RV function
options.C: Start sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor) — pulmonary arterial hypertension medications are indicated for cor pulmonale from COPD
options.D: Start digoxin for RV failure — RV failure in COPD responds to positive inotropic agents

questions.rationale: Cor pulmonale (RV hypertrophy and failure secondary to pulmonary hypertension from lung disease) in COPD: the primary driver is chronic hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (SpO2 86% — meets LTOT criteria). The PRIMARY treatment is supplemental oxygen — it reverses hypoxic vasoconstriction, reduces pulmonary artery pressure, and improves RV function and survival. Diuresis (option A) treats volume overload (edema) symptomatically but does not address the underlying pulmonary hypertension — it is adjunctive, not primary. Sildenafil (option C) is used in idiopathic pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH), NOT for pulmonary hypertension secondary to COPD — current evidence shows no benefit and possible harm in COPD-related PH. Digoxin (option D) is not a proven therapy for RV failure in COPD and carries significant toxicity risk.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + ICS + recurrent pneumonia — reassess ICS need based on eosinophil count and exacerbation type
questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with COPD (GOLD 3, Group E) on umeclidinium/vilanterol/fluticasone triple therapy has had 2 pneumonias in the past 12 months (confirmed on CXR). His blood eosinophil count is 85 cells/μL. He has had 2 COPD exacerbations (treated with antibiotics + prednisone) in the same period. Which of the following represents the most appropriate medication adjustment?

options.A: Continue triple therapy — ICS is essential for all Group E COPD patients regardless of complications
options.B: Discontinue the ICS component — eosinophil count <100 cells/μL suggests ICS-responsive inflammation is unlikely; pneumonia risk may outweigh benefit in this patient
options.C: Add another ICS (beclomethasone) for additional anti-inflammatory coverage to prevent further exacerbations
options.D: Switch to SABA + SAMA only — all inhaled medications carry pneumonia risk in COPD

questions.rationale: ICS in COPD increases pneumonia risk by approximately 3% absolute (NNH approximately 33). The ICS benefit in COPD is most evident in patients with blood eosinophils ≥300 cells/μL — these patients have an eosinophilic/asthmatic component that is ICS-responsive. This patient has eosinophils of only 85 cells/μL — at this low level, ICS benefit is uncertain while the pneumonia risk is real (2 pneumonias in 12 months). GOLD 2023 guidance: consider ICS discontinuation if eosinophils <100 cells/μL, especially if recurrent pneumonia is occurring. Step-down: switch to LAMA + LABA (dual bronchodilation without ICS). Monitor exacerbation frequency after ICS withdrawal — if exacerbations worsen, may need to add ICS back. Adding a second ICS (option C) is never appropriate. SABA + SAMA only (option D) would massively undertreat this severe patient.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + 5-day prednisone course evidence — REDUCE trial duration
questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman with COPD (GOLD 2, Group E) has her third outpatient exacerbation this year. You are prescribing a short course of oral corticosteroids. She asks why you are only prescribing 5 days of prednisone when she previously received 10-day courses from an urgent care. Which of the following correctly explains the current evidence-based duration?

options.A: 10-day courses are still required — shorter courses fail to adequately reduce airway inflammation
options.B: 5-day prednisone 40 mg/day is equivalent to 14-day courses for COPD exacerbations — REDUCE trial (2013); shorter courses reduce total steroid exposure without worse outcomes
options.C: A single-dose injection of methylprednisolone is sufficient for COPD exacerbations
options.D: Prednisone for more than 3 days causes adrenal suppression and must be tapered regardless of duration

questions.rationale: The REDUCE trial (2013, New England Journal of Medicine): in hospitalized COPD exacerbation patients, a 5-day course of prednisone 40 mg daily was non-inferior to a 14-day course for time to next exacerbation, spirometry recovery, and clinical outcomes — with significantly less cumulative steroid exposure. This has been extrapolated to outpatient exacerbations: standard of care is now prednisone 40 mg × 5 days. Benefits of shorter course: less hyperglycemia, fewer steroid myopathy events, reduced adrenal suppression risk. Option D (taper requirement): a 5-day course at 40 mg does not typically require tapering in patients without prior adrenal suppression. However, frequent short courses (≥3/year) may warrant consideration of inhaled steroid optimization to reduce systemic steroid use.

questions.stemAnchor: COPD + palliative care discussion — goals of care advanced COPD dyspnea management
questions.stem: A 79-year-old man with GOLD 4 COPD (FEV1 22%, 4 hospitalizations in 18 months, SpO2 85% at rest on 3L O2) has declining function — now bedbound with severe dyspnea at minimal activity. He has indicated he does not want intubation or ICU care. His son asks you to 'do everything possible.' Which of the following best represents the most appropriate approach?

options.A: Admit to ICU immediately and initiate mechanical ventilation as the standard of care
options.B: Engage in goals-of-care conversation with patient and family; optimize comfort measures: low-dose oral morphine (reduces dyspnea), supplemental oxygen, palliative care/hospice referral if goals shift to comfort focus
options.C: Do not discuss goals of care — it will frighten the patient and family
options.D: Continue to maximize COPD medical therapy including adding azithromycin and roflumilast; avoid palliative care referral as it signals giving up

questions.rationale: Advanced COPD with severe dyspnea despite maximal therapy, frequent hospitalizations, and the patient's expressed wish not to be intubated: this patient meets criteria for goals-of-care discussion and palliative care integration. Evidence-based dyspnea management in advanced COPD: low-dose oral opioids (morphine immediate release 2.5–5 mg every 4 hours) reduce dyspnea perception significantly without consistently causing respiratory depression at appropriate doses. Palliative care does NOT mean stopping treatment — it means addressing comfort alongside disease management. The NP's role: facilitate the conversation, clarify values (quality vs length of life), document advance directives, and refer to palliative care or hospice when appropriate. Continuing to maximize pharmacological COPD therapy (option D) is appropriate but without addressing goals of care and comfort management, the patient's quality of life remains unaddressed.

questions.stemAnchor: 64-year-old + 10-year ASCVD risk 12% + LDL 142 + no established ASCVD — statin intensity selection primary prevention
questions.stem: A 64-year-old man has a 10-year ASCVD risk of 12% (calculated using the pooled cohort equation). LDL is 142 mg/dL, HDL 38 mg/dL. He has no established ASCVD, no DM, and no FH (LDL <190). He is a former smoker (quit 3 years ago) and has hypertension on amlodipine. Which of the following most accurately represents the 2018 ACC/AHA statin recommendation for this patient?

options.A: High-intensity statin — ASCVD risk >10% always requires high-intensity statin
options.B: Moderate-intensity statin — 10-year ASCVD risk 7.5–20% in adults 40–75 years = moderate-intensity statin (consider high-intensity if risk >20%)
options.C: No statin indicated — lifestyle modification only is recommended for ASCVD risk <15%
options.D: Low-intensity statin — minimizes side effects while providing cardiovascular protection

questions.rationale: 2018 ACC/AHA Cholesterol guidelines: For primary prevention in adults 40–75 without established ASCVD, T2DM, or FH: (1) If 10-year ASCVD risk ≥7.5% → moderate-intensity statin; (2) If risk ≥20% → high-intensity statin or moderate + risk discussion for high-intensity. This patient has a 12% risk — moderate-intensity statin is indicated (e.g., atorvastatin 10–20 mg, rosuvastatin 5–10 mg, simvastatin 20–40 mg). The guidelines also recommend discussing additional risk factors (risk-enhancing factors) such as: LDL ≥160, family history, high-sensitivity CRP ≥2 mg/L, CAC score, ABI ≤0.9. If the risk-benefit conversation still leaves uncertainty, a CAC score can guide the decision. Option A incorrectly mandates high-intensity for >10% risk.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient on simvastatin + amiodarone + AV dilation — statin-drug interaction CYP3A4
questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with AF and dyslipidemia has been on simvastatin 40 mg daily for 3 years. His cardiologist started amiodarone 200 mg daily 4 months ago. He now presents with bilateral thigh and calf muscle pain and weakness. CK is 8,200 U/L (normal <200). Creatinine is 1.6 mg/dL (was 1.1). Which of the following best explains this presentation and most appropriate action?

options.A: Amiodarone-induced peripheral neuropathy — continue both medications and add gabapentin
options.B: Simvastatin-amiodarone drug interaction: amiodarone inhibits CYP3A4, causing simvastatin accumulation and myotoxicity; hold simvastatin immediately and hydrate; switch to a non-CYP3A4 metabolized statin (rosuvastatin or pravastatin)
options.C: Hypokalemia from amiodarone — check electrolytes and supplement potassium
options.D: Statin myopathy unrelated to amiodarone — switch to atorvastatin which is safer

questions.rationale: Simvastatin is primarily metabolized by CYP3A4. Amiodarone is a moderate-to-strong CYP3A4 inhibitor — it significantly reduces simvastatin metabolism, causing drug accumulation and myotoxicity. FDA black-box warning: simvastatin 80 mg is prohibited with amiodarone; simvastatin at any dose combined with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors carries myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk. CK 8,200 U/L = myositis (>10× ULN = 2,000) — approaching rhabdomyolysis. Rising creatinine suggests myoglobinuria-related AKI. Immediate actions: (1) Hold simvastatin, (2) Aggressive IV/oral hydration, (3) Monitor urine output, CK, creatinine, and electrolytes, (4) Switch to rosuvastatin or pravastatin (not CYP3A4-metabolized — safer with amiodarone). Atorvastatin (option D) is also CYP3A4-metabolized and carries the same interaction risk with amiodarone.

questions.stemAnchor: LDL 218 mg/dL in 32-year-old + no ASCVD + father had MI at 46 — familial hypercholesterolemia recognition
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents for a new patient visit. Her LDL is 218 mg/dL; HDL 52 mg/dL; TG 112 mg/dL. She is not on any medications. BMI is 23 and she does not smoke. She reports her father had a myocardial infarction at age 46 and her paternal grandfather had a stroke at 50. She has no tendon xanthomas. Her 10-year ASCVD risk is only 2.5% (young age). Which of the following best characterizes her lipid disorder and appropriate management?

options.A: No treatment needed — 10-year ASCVD risk of 2.5% is low; lifestyle modification only
options.B: Familial hypercholesterolemia (FH) — LDL ≥190 at any age + family history of premature ASCVD is a compelling indication for high-intensity statin regardless of calculated 10-year risk
options.C: Start low-intensity statin (rosuvastatin 5 mg) — she is young and low-risk
options.D: Order lipoprotein (a) — this is the only test needed before starting any therapy

questions.rationale: Familial Hypercholesterolemia (FH): LDL ≥190 mg/dL in an adult + family history of premature ASCVD (father MI at 46 = premature — <55 in men) is the clinical diagnosis. FH is an autosomal dominant condition causing lifelong LDL elevation and dramatically accelerated ASCVD risk — the calculated 10-year risk grossly UNDERESTIMATES lifetime risk. Management: HIGH-INTENSITY statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg or rosuvastatin 20–40 mg) regardless of calculated short-term risk. Additional considerations: genetic testing, cascade screening of first-degree relatives, LDL target <70 mg/dL (or >50% reduction from baseline). If max statin insufficient: add ezetimibe; if still not at goal: PCSK9 inhibitor. A 10-year risk calculation (option A) is inappropriate as the primary decision-making tool in FH — lifetime risk perspective is required. Rosuvastatin 5 mg (low-intensity, option C) is inadequate for LDL 218 with FH.

questions.stemAnchor: Patient with statin myalgia + normal CK — statin-attributed myalgia vs confirmation with drug rechallenge
questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman was started on atorvastatin 40 mg for primary prevention (10-year ASCVD risk 9%). After 6 weeks, she reports bilateral leg muscle aching that is interfering with her daily walks. CK is 145 U/L (normal). She has no objective weakness. She asks to stop the statin. Which of the following best represents the most appropriate management?

options.A: Confirm statin-attributed myopathy and permanently discontinue all statins — CK elevation is required for a statin myopathy diagnosis
options.B: Hold atorvastatin for 4–6 weeks; if symptoms resolve, rechallenge with atorvastatin 20 mg or try rosuvastatin or pravastatin (different statin); statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS) with normal CK are common and often manageable
options.C: Continue atorvastatin — muscle aching with normal CK is not statin-related
options.D: Add CoQ10 (ubiquinone) 200 mg daily — CoQ10 deficiency from statin use is the proven cause of statin myalgia

questions.rationale: Statin-associated muscle symptoms (SAMS): myalgia with normal CK occurs in approximately 5–10% of statin users. CK elevation is NOT required for a diagnosis of SAMS — myalgia with normal CK still may be statin-related. The statin causality rule: symptoms resolve within 4–6 weeks of discontinuation and return with rechallenge → confirms statin attribution. Management of SAMS: (1) Temporary discontinuation + re-challenge (confirms attribution), (2) Dose reduction, (3) Switching to a different statin (rosuvastatin and pravastatin are associated with lower myalgia rates — less lipophilic), (4) Alternate-day dosing for long-half-life statins (rosuvastatin). CoQ10 supplementation (option D) has not shown consistent benefit in clinical trials for SAMS — some observational data suggests partial benefit, but it is not evidence-based as a recommendation. Permanently stopping statins without rechallenge (option A) deprives the patient of significant ASCVD benefit.

questions.stemAnchor: High-risk ASCVD + LDL 88 on max statin — add ezetimibe IMPROVE-IT evidence
questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with established ASCVD (MI 2 years ago) is on atorvastatin 80 mg daily. His LDL is 88 mg/dL. Per 2018 ACC/AHA guidelines, his LDL target is <70 mg/dL (and <55 mg/dL as a 'very high risk' optional target). He asks about additional options to further reduce his LDL. Which of the following is the appropriate next step?

options.A: No additional therapy — LDL 88 mg/dL on high-intensity statin is optimal
options.B: Add ezetimibe 10 mg — the IMPROVE-IT trial demonstrated that ezetimibe + statin reduced CV events in post-ACS patients; adds 15–20% additional LDL reduction
options.C: Switch to a moderate-intensity statin (rosuvastatin 10 mg) — high-intensity statin is excessive for this LDL level
options.D: Add niacin extended-release for additional LDL lowering — HPS2-THRIVE evidence

questions.rationale: Ezetimibe is the appropriate add-on when LDL remains above target on maximum tolerated statin.
