IMPROVE-IT trial: in post-ACS patients on simvastatin 40 mg, adding ezetimibe 10 mg reduced LDL an additional 15–20% (from ~69 to ~54 mg/dL) and reduced the composite CV outcome by 6.4% relative risk reduction over 7 years. This patient's LDL is 88 mg/dL on atorvastatin 80 mg — above the <70 mg/dL target (and above <55 mg/dL very high risk optional target). Adding ezetimibe is the guideline-recommended second step before PCSK9 inhibitors. Switching to a moderate-intensity statin (option C) would INCREASE LDL, not decrease it. Niacin (option D): HPS2-THRIVE and AIM-HIGH trials showed no additional CV benefit from niacin added to effective statin therapy — niacin is no longer recommended for secondary prevention in statin-treated patients.

questions.stemAnchor: 34-year-old woman + statin prescription + wants to get pregnant — statin contraindication in pregnancy
questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman with familial hypercholesterolemia (LDL 242 mg/dL) is being counseled about statin therapy. She is currently trying to conceive (she and her partner have been trying for 3 months). Which of the following correctly describes the management of her statin therapy in this context?

options.A: Start atorvastatin 40 mg immediately — the benefit of LDL reduction outweighs risks
options.B: Delay all LDL-lowering therapy until she is no longer trying to conceive or is done breastfeeding — all statins are contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding; ezetimibe is also not recommended in pregnancy
options.C: Start ezetimibe only — ezetimibe is the only lipid-lowering agent safe in pregnancy
options.D: Start bile acid sequestrant (cholestyramine) — this is the only pregnancy-safe class of lipid-lowering medications

questions.rationale: All statins are Category X in pregnancy — associated with teratogenicity (skeletal and CNS malformations in animal studies). They must be discontinued before pregnancy attempts. Ezetimibe (option C) has not been studied in human pregnancy and is not recommended. Bile acid sequestrants (option D) are considered relatively safer in pregnancy (do not cross the placenta), but their benefit in FH is modest and they are not routinely recommended for primary lipid management in this context. In practice: for patients with FH who are planning pregnancy, the approach is to stop statins, optimize lifestyle, and use bile acid sequestrants if treatment is deemed necessary during pregnancy — but since this patient is trying to conceive, statins should be stopped NOW and not restarted until after breastfeeding is complete. PCSK9 inhibitors also lack pregnancy safety data.

questions.stemAnchor: Fasting TG 720 mg/dL + pancreatitis risk — omega-3 icosapent ethyl Vascepa vs fibrate
questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with T2DM and ASCVD has a fasting triglyceride of 720 mg/dL. He is on atorvastatin 40 mg, metformin, and empagliflozin. LDL is 68 mg/dL. He has no prior pancreatitis. Which of the following is the most appropriate additional medication for his severe hypertriglyceridemia?

options.A: Add fenofibrate — fibrates are the most evidence-based therapy for severe hypertriglyceridemia to prevent pancreatitis
options.B: Add icosapent ethyl (Vascepa) 4g daily — REDUCE-IT trial demonstrated CV benefit in statin-treated patients with TG ≥135 mg/dL; also effective for TG reduction
options.C: Add niacin — best tolerated lipid-lowering agent for hypertriglyceridemia
options.D: Stop atorvastatin — statins raise triglycerides

questions.rationale: For severe hypertriglyceridemia (≥500 mg/dL), pancreatitis prevention is the priority. However, this patient has ASCVD, and icosapent ethyl (IPE/Vascepa) addresses BOTH goals: REDUCE-IT trial demonstrated significant CV event reduction (25% relative risk reduction) in patients with TG ≥135 mg/dL on statin therapy with established ASCVD or DM + risk factors. This patient's profile (T2DM + ASCVD + TG 720 + on statin with LDL at goal) is the ideal REDUCE-IT candidate. Fenofibrate (option A) is effective for TG reduction but has not demonstrated CV benefit in statin-treated patients (ACCORD-Lipid, FIELD trials). It is appropriate if IPE is unavailable or contraindicated, but IPE is preferred for CV benefit. Niacin (option C) is no longer recommended. Statins (option D) generally reduce TG by 10–30% — they should be continued.

questions.stemAnchor: PCSK9 inhibitor indication — refractory FH not at goal on max statin + ezetimibe
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with FH and established ASCVD (prior MI) is on atorvastatin 80 mg + ezetimibe 10 mg. LDL remains 112 mg/dL. His LDL goal is <70 mg/dL (ideally <55 mg/dL for very high risk). He asks about 'the shots' he's read about online. Which of the following correctly identifies the therapy and its mechanism?

options.A: These are PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab/alirocumab) — monoclonal antibodies that inhibit PCSK9, increasing LDL receptor recycling on hepatocytes; reduce LDL by 50–60% on top of statin + ezetimibe
options.B: These are GLP-1 receptor agonists — they reduce cardiovascular events and lower LDL as a secondary effect
options.C: These are SGLT-2 inhibitors — they improve cardiovascular outcomes and lower LDL
options.D: These are anti-inflammatory biologics — inflammation reduction lowers LDL levels in statin-resistant patients

questions.rationale: PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab — Repatha, alirocumab — Praluent): subcutaneous injections every 2 weeks or monthly. Mechanism: PCSK9 (proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9) normally degrades LDL receptors on hepatocytes — inhibiting PCSK9 increases LDL receptor recycling → more LDL removed from circulation. Effect: reduces LDL by 50–60% on top of maximally tolerated statin + ezetimibe. Indications: FH (especially heterozygous FH), established ASCVD with LDL not at goal on maximally tolerated statin + ezetimibe. FDA-approved; demonstrated CV benefit in FOURIER (evolocumab) and ODYSSEY OUTCOMES (alirocumab) trials. Limitation: very high cost (~$4,000–6,000/year); insurance authorization often required. This patient meets criteria: FH + established ASCVD + LDL not at goal despite max statin + ezetimibe.

questions.stemAnchor: 48-year-old with new statin + new DM diagnosis — statin-induced hyperglycemia monitoring
questions.stem: A 48-year-old woman with no prior diabetes history (fasting glucose 98 mg/dL at baseline) was started on atorvastatin 40 mg 6 months ago for primary prevention (10-year risk 8%). At her 6-month follow-up, fasting glucose is 118 mg/dL and HbA1c is 6.1%. She is not taking any other medications and has not changed her diet. She asks if her statin 'gave her diabetes.' Which of the following is the most accurate and appropriate response?

options.A: Discontinue atorvastatin — statins cause diabetes and should not be used in pre-diabetic patients
options.B: Statins have a small but real association with increased incident T2DM, especially in patients with risk factors (pre-diabetes); the CV benefit of statins significantly outweighs this modest risk; continue statin, counsel on lifestyle modification, and monitor glucose
options.C: The glucose elevation is unrelated to statin therapy — statins are metabolically neutral
options.D: Switch to pravastatin — it has no diabetogenic effect unlike atorvastatin

questions.rationale: Statin and diabetes: class effect — all statins are associated with a modest increase in incident T2DM (approximately 10–12% relative risk increase; approximately 1 additional diabetes case per 100 patients over 4 years at high-dose statin). Risk is highest with: high-intensity statins, pre-existing impaired fasting glucose (this patient's baseline glucose 98 was already pre-diabetic range). The WOSCOPS, JUPITER, and meta-analyses confirm this association. The key teaching point: the CV benefit of statins (preventing MIs and strokes) vastly outweighs the modest diabetes risk — statins should NOT be discontinued. The correct approach: (1) Continue statin, (2) Counsel on lifestyle (diet, exercise, weight management) to prevent progression to T2DM, (3) Monitor glucose annually, (4) If T2DM develops, manage appropriately. Pravastatin (option D) has slightly less diabetogenic effect but the difference is clinically marginal.

questions.stemAnchor: CAC score 0 in 62-year-old borderline risk — statin deferral based on CAC
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with hypertension has a 10-year ASCVD risk of 9.8% (borderline intermediate risk). He has no established ASCVD. LDL is 128 mg/dL. He is uncertain about starting a statin. A coronary artery calcium (CAC) score is obtained as a risk discussion aid. His CAC score is 0. Which of the following correctly describes how the CAC score should influence the statin decision?

options.A: CAC 0 strongly argues against initiating statin therapy in this borderline-risk patient — it indicates minimal calcified plaque burden; statin initiation can be reasonably deferred
options.B: CAC 0 does not change the recommendation — intermediate-risk patients always require a statin
options.C: CAC 0 means the patient has no ASCVD risk and statin therapy is contraindicated
options.D: CAC 0 indicates soft plaque is predominant and statin therapy should be started immediately

questions.rationale: Coronary artery calcium (CAC) scoring is a risk-discussion aid endorsed by the 2018 ACC/AHA Cholesterol guidelines. In borderline risk (7.5–20%) patients where statin decision is uncertain, CAC = 0 provides strong evidence to DEFER statin therapy: patients with CAC 0 have a 10-year ASCVD event rate approaching that of the general population without intervention. Meta-analyses show CAC 0 is associated with a negative predictive value >99% for cardiac events over 10 years. The recommendation: 'If the patient is in the statin-uncertain zone and CAC = 0, it is reasonable to withhold statin therapy and reassess in 5 years.' Re-scan CAC in 5 years. Caveat: CAC 0 does NOT guarantee no risk — patients with very high LDL (e.g., FH with LDL ≥190) or strong family history should still be treated regardless of CAC score. This patient's LDL 128 does not trigger that exception.

questions.stemAnchor: IDA + oral iron + GI side effects + poor adherence — alternate-day dosing strategy Moretti trial
questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with heavy menstrual bleeding has iron-deficiency anemia (Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 72 fL, ferritin 6 ng/mL). She was started on ferrous sulfate 325 mg three times daily but returns 4 weeks later with minimal improvement — Hgb 10.1 g/dL. She reports she has been taking only 1 tablet daily instead of 3 because of nausea and constipation. Which of the following is the most evidence-based recommendation to improve iron absorption with better tolerability?

options.A: Continue three times daily dosing and add a proton pump inhibitor to reduce GI side effects
options.B: Switch to alternate-day ferrous sulfate dosing — the Moretti trial demonstrated that every-other-day iron dosing achieves equivalent or superior total iron absorption as daily dosing with fewer GI side effects (by avoiding hepcidin upregulation)
options.C: Stop oral iron and immediately switch to IV iron (iron sucrose) since oral iron is ineffective in IDA
options.D: Add folic acid 1 mg daily — IDA is often accompanied by folate deficiency that reduces absorption

questions.rationale: The Moretti trial (2015, Blood) demonstrated a counterintuitive finding: alternate-day oral iron dosing achieves EQUAL or SUPERIOR total fractional iron absorption compared to consecutive daily dosing. Mechanism: oral iron intake stimulates hepcidin production within 24 hours — hepcidin blocks ferroportin (the intestinal iron transporter), reducing absorption of the next dose. By dosing every other day, the hepcidin peak has cleared, allowing the next dose to be absorbed more efficiently. Clinical benefit: equivalent efficacy with FEWER GI side effects (nausea, constipation) — improving adherence. This is now a recommended option for IDA. IV iron (option C) is reserved for: malabsorption, GI intolerance despite alternate-day dosing, ongoing blood loss exceeding oral supplementation, or eGFR <30. PPI (option A) does not meaningfully reduce iron side effects.

questions.stemAnchor: Macrocytic anemia + B12 deficiency + strict vegan + paresthesias — subacute combined degeneration treatment urgency
questions.stem: A 42-year-old strict vegan woman presents with fatigue, difficulty walking, and bilateral hand tingling for 3 months. CBC shows: Hgb 9.4 g/dL, MCV 112 fL. Peripheral smear: hypersegmented neutrophils. B12 level is 88 pg/mL (low; normal >200). Folate is normal. Neurological exam: impaired vibration sense in both feet and positive Romberg test. She has no intrinsic factor antibodies. Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment?

options.A: Folic acid 1 mg daily — macrocytic anemia in a vegan is most likely folate deficiency
options.B: High-dose oral cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily OR intramuscular B12 injections — neurological B12 deficiency requires urgent repletion; high-dose oral B12 can be effective even without intrinsic factor via passive absorption
options.C: Dietary counseling only — switching to a B12-fortified diet will resolve the deficiency within 2 weeks
options.D: Wait 8 weeks for intramuscular B12 to work — neurological symptoms will resolve slowly regardless of route

questions.rationale: B12 deficiency with neurological involvement (subacute combined degeneration — posterior column: vibration/proprioception loss, lateral column: Romberg positive) is a neurological emergency requiring prompt repletion. Key distinction: impaired vibration sense and ataxia can become irreversible if untreated. Treatment: (1) Intramuscular B12 (cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg IM) is traditionally used for malabsorption (pernicious anemia with absent intrinsic factor — this patient has no IF antibodies, suggesting dietary deficiency not PA); (2) High-dose oral B12 (1000 mcg/day) is effective even in pernicious anemia via passive diffusion (1% of ingested B12 is absorbed passively without intrinsic factor) — multiple studies confirm oral high-dose is equivalent to IM for PA; (3) For a vegan, dietary supplementation correction is too slow for neurological deficits — medical repletion is required. Option A (folic acid): CRITICAL — never give folic acid alone without first replenishing B12 in a patient with neurological deficits. Folic acid treats the anemia but unmasks/accelerates B12 neuropathy.

questions.stemAnchor: 62-year-old + macrocytic anemia + alcohol use disorder + liver disease — alcohol vs B12 vs folate vs hypothyroid differential
questions.stem: A 62-year-old man with alcohol use disorder (2–3 pints of vodka daily for 20 years) presents with fatigue. CBC: Hgb 9.6 g/dL, MCV 108 fL, WBC 4.2, platelets 98,000. Liver enzymes: AST 124, ALT 58, alkaline phosphatase 186, GGT 340. B12 is 420 pg/mL (normal). Folate is 3.4 ng/mL (low). Peripheral smear shows macroovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils. TSH is normal. Which of the following is the correct diagnosis and management?

options.A: B12 deficiency — the elevated MCV requires B12 supplementation
options.B: Alcohol-induced macrocytosis — alcohol directly toxifies erythrocyte precursors; no specific treatment beyond alcohol cessation
options.C: Folate deficiency from alcohol use disorder (poor intake + alcohol-folate interaction) — treat with folic acid 1 mg daily; address alcohol use; check B12 before starting folic acid
options.D: Aplastic anemia from alcohol toxicity — platelet count suggests bone marrow suppression requiring colony-stimulating factor

questions.rationale: This presentation shows: macrocytic anemia + low folate + elevated GGT (alcohol-specific) + B12 normal + hypersegmented neutrophils (folate deficiency morphology). Alcohol causes folate deficiency through: (1) poor dietary intake, (2) impaired intestinal folate absorption, (3) increased folate excretion. The folate level 3.4 ng/mL is low (normal >5.4). Hypersegmented neutrophils are a classic morphological finding in folate (and B12) deficiency. Management: (1) Confirm B12 is adequate (done — normal) before starting folic acid; (2) Folic acid 1 mg daily; (3) Alcohol cessation counseling and treatment. The pancytopenia (low platelets) is from hypersplenism (portal hypertension from cirrhosis) and bone marrow suppression from alcohol — not aplastic anemia. Alcohol itself also causes macrocytosis independently of folate (MCV normalizes with cessation), but the hypersegmented neutrophils specifically indicate nutrient deficiency.

questions.stemAnchor: G6PD deficiency + primaquine triggered hemolysis — X-linked, Heinz bodies, oxidant avoidance
questions.stem: A 28-year-old man of West African descent was started on primaquine for malaria prophylaxis prior to travel. 5 days later he develops dark urine, jaundice, and fatigue. CBC: Hgb 7.2 g/dL (was 13.8), MCV 90, reticulocyte count 8.2% (elevated). LDH 680 U/L (elevated). Peripheral smear: bite cells (degmacytes) and Heinz bodies on crystal violet stain. Which of the following correctly identifies the diagnosis and management?

options.A: Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) — positive direct Coombs test confirms; start prednisone
options.B: G6PD deficiency — X-linked enzymatic deficiency; hemolytic crisis triggered by oxidant drug (primaquine); discontinue primaquine; provide supportive care; test family members
options.C: Sickle cell crisis precipitated by malaria — start IV fluids and opioids
options.D: Hereditary spherocytosis — osmotic fragility test confirms; splenectomy is curative

questions.rationale: G6PD deficiency: X-linked (predominantly males), prevalent in malaria-endemic regions (West Africa, Mediterranean, Southeast Asia). G6PD protects RBCs from oxidant stress — deficient RBCs cannot maintain glutathione, leading to hemoglobin oxidation. Classic precipitants: primaquine, dapsone, nitrofurantoin, sulfonamides, rasburicase, fava beans, and infections. Presentation: acute intravascular hemolysis — dark urine (hemoglobinuria), jaundice, rapid Hgb drop, elevated LDH and reticulocytes. Characteristic smear: Heinz bodies (denatured Hgb precipitates — seen on crystal violet stain) + bite cells (splenic macrophages have 'bitten' out the Heinz bodies). Management: discontinue the oxidant drug, supportive care, transfuse if Hgb <7 with symptoms. Screen family members. AIHA (option A) shows positive direct Coombs test and does not involve Heinz bodies. Spherocytosis (option D) shows spherocytes on smear, not bite cells.

questions.stemAnchor: TTP pentad — MAHA + thrombocytopenia + fever + AKI + neuro changes — plasma exchange emergency
questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with confusion and fever for 2 days. Labs: Hgb 7.8 g/dL, platelets 28,000/mm³, creatinine 2.4 mg/dL (was 0.9 last year). Peripheral smear shows fragmented RBCs (schistocytes). LDH is 1,280 U/L. She has no history of lupus. Coagulation studies (PT, PTT) are normal. Direct Coombs test is negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate action?

options.A: Administer platelet transfusion immediately — thrombocytopenia with bleeding risk requires platelet replacement
options.B: Start broad-spectrum antibiotics — this presentation is consistent with bacterial sepsis with DIC
options.C: Call hematology/oncology urgently — thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) diagnosis requires emergent plasma exchange (PEX), which is life-saving; avoid platelet transfusion
options.D: Start high-dose prednisone and rituximab for immune thrombocytopenic purpura (ITP)

questions.rationale: Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura (TTP): the classic pentad (not all need to be present) — Microangiopathic Hemolytic Anemia (MAHA: schistocytes + elevated LDH + low Hgb + negative Coombs) + Thrombocytopenia + Fever + Acute Kidney Injury + Neurological symptoms (confusion). Mechanism: ADAMTS13 protease deficiency → ultra-large vWF multimers → platelet thrombi in microvasculature. CRITICAL: Platelet transfusion is CONTRAINDICATED in TTP — it 'fuels the fire,' providing more substrate for thrombosis and worsening outcomes. Emergency treatment: plasma exchange (PEX) with fresh frozen plasma is life-saving (removes auto-antibodies, replaces ADAMTS13). DIC (option B) would show abnormal PT/PTT — normal here. ITP (option D) causes isolated thrombocytopenia without MAHA. The NP must immediately transfer this patient to a tertiary center with apheresis capability.

questions.stemAnchor: Anemia of chronic disease vs IDA — differentiation using ferritin and serum iron
questions.stem: A 62-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis (on methotrexate) and CKD G3a presents with fatigue and pallor. CBC: Hgb 9.6 g/dL, MCV 82 fL. Iron studies: serum iron 48 mcg/dL (low), TIBC 195 (low-normal), ferritin 210 ng/mL (elevated), TSAT 19%. Which of the following most accurately describes this anemia type and appropriate initial management?

options.A: Iron-deficiency anemia — low serum iron confirms iron deficiency; start ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily
options.B: Anemia of chronic disease (ACD) — pattern: low serum iron + low-normal TIBC + elevated ferritin (stored iron is high, but trapped in RES macrophages by hepcidin); the primary treatment is addressing the underlying chronic disease
options.C: B12 deficiency from methotrexate — macrocytic anemia expected; recheck MCV and B12 level
options.D: Aplastic anemia from methotrexate — CBC shows only mild anemia, ruling this out

questions.rationale: Anemia of Chronic Disease (ACD) pattern: (1) Low serum iron (iron trapped in macrophages by hepcidin), (2) Low to normal TIBC (inflammation suppresses transferrin synthesis), (3) Elevated ferritin (acute phase reactant — elevated in inflammation even when true iron stores are adequate), (4) MCV normal or mildly low. This is the OPPOSITE of IDA: IDA = low ferritin + high TIBC. The key distinguishing feature: ferritin >100 + low serum iron = ACD (or iron deficiency CONCURRENT with ACD — 'functional iron deficiency'). In this patient with RA and CKD, ACD is the primary mechanism. Treatment: manage the underlying disease (RA flare control with methotrexate/biologics); if ferritin <30 or TSAT <20% despite elevated ferritin, consider IV iron (particularly in CKD). ESA (erythropoiesis-stimulating agents) may be considered if Hgb <10 in CKD. Ferrous sulfate (option A) is not effective in ACD with adequate stores.

questions.stemAnchor: Warm AIHA + positive direct Coombs + lupus — first-line prednisone treatment
questions.stem: A 38-year-old woman with SLE presents with progressive fatigue and jaundice over 3 weeks. CBC: Hgb 7.4 g/dL, MCV 96, reticulocyte count 6.8% (elevated). LDH 520 U/L. Direct Coombs test (DAT): positive (IgG). Peripheral smear shows spherocytes. Haptoglobin is undetectable (<10 mg/dL). Which of the following correctly identifies the diagnosis and first-line treatment?

options.A: Cold agglutinin disease — IgM-mediated; treat with rituximab
options.B: Warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) — IgG-mediated, positive DAT (IgG), extravascular hemolysis; first-line treatment is corticosteroids (prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day)
options.C: TTP — positive DAT with schistocytes; emergent plasma exchange required
options.D: G6PD deficiency — Heinz bodies on smear; stop offending medications

questions.rationale: Warm AIHA: IgG autoantibodies bind RBCs at body temperature → extravascular hemolysis by splenic macrophages → spherocytes on smear (macrophages remove portions of RBC membrane) + positive direct Coombs for IgG. Associated with SLE (most common autoimmune association), CLL, lymphoma, and certain drugs (methyldopa, penicillin, cephalosporins). First-line treatment: prednisone 1–2 mg/kg/day until Hgb >10 g/dL and reticulocytes respond → taper over 2–3 months. Second-line if steroid-refractory: rituximab, splenectomy, other immunosuppressants. Cold agglutinin disease (option A) is IgM-mediated, positive DAT for complement (C3d), worsens with cold exposure — different clinical context. TTP (option C): spherocytes are NOT the typical schistocyte pattern of TTP, and positive DAT would not be present. G6PD (option D): Heinz bodies with negative DAT.

questions.stemAnchor: Sickle cell anemia + acute chest syndrome — recognition as emergency requiring transfusion
questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with known sickle cell disease (HbSS) presents with fever (38.9°C), chest pain, and worsening hypoxia (SpO2 88% on room air). CXR shows a new bilateral infiltrate in the lower lobes. His Hgb is 6.2 g/dL (his baseline Hgb is 8.5 g/dL). He has been hospitalized twice in 2 years for vaso-occlusive crises. Which of the following represents the most appropriate management?

options.A: Outpatient antibiotics for community-acquired pneumonia and return precautions
options.B: Call 911 immediately — acute chest syndrome (ACS) requires hospital admission for oxygen supplementation, exchange transfusion or simple transfusion, antibiotics (atypical coverage), and aggressive pain management
options.C: Administer high-dose hydroxyurea — this prevents acute chest syndrome in the chronic setting
options.D: Arrange elective outpatient hematology referral within 2 weeks

questions.rationale: Acute Chest Syndrome (ACS) in sickle cell disease: NEW pulmonary infiltrate + one or more of: fever, respiratory symptoms (chest pain, cough, dyspnea), new hypoxia. ACS is the leading cause of death in sickle cell disease. This patient has the full picture: fever + chest pain + new bilateral infiltrate + hypoxia + Hgb drop below baseline. Management (hospital, often ICU): (1) Supplemental oxygen to maintain SpO2 ≥95%; (2) Exchange transfusion or simple transfusion to reduce HbS% (<30% with exchange transfusion) — reduces vascular occlusion; (3) Antibiotics covering typical and atypical organisms (ceftriaxone + azithromycin — atypical coverage critical as Mycoplasma and Chlamydophila are common triggers); (4) Aggressive IV hydration; (5) Pain management. Hydroxyurea (option C) is chronic preventive therapy — it PREVENTS future ACS but has no role in acute management.

questions.stemAnchor: Normocytic anemia + hypothyroidism + CKD — differentiating mixed anemia causes
questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman with known hypothyroidism (on levothyroxine 88 mcg, TSH 6.8 — undertreated), CKD G3a (eGFR 48 mL/min), and osteoarthritis presents with fatigue. CBC: Hgb 10.1 g/dL, MCV 87 fL (normocytic), reticulocyte count 1.2% (normal-low). Ferritin 180 ng/mL (normal), TIBC normal, B12 420 pg/mL (normal), folate normal. EPO level is 8.4 mU/mL (inappropriately low for this Hgb). TSH 6.8 mIU/L. Which of the following best characterizes the primary contributing causes of her anemia?

options.A: Iron-deficiency anemia — start oral ferrous sulfate
options.B: Normocytic anemia from BOTH undertreated hypothyroidism (thyroid hormone required for erythropoiesis) and renal anemia (inadequate EPO response) — optimize levothyroxine dose; monitor Hgb response before initiating ESA
options.C: Anemia of chronic disease from osteoarthritis — NSAIDs are causing GI blood loss
options.D: B12 deficiency — MCV 87 rules out B12 deficiency as a contributor

questions.rationale: This patient has normocytic anemia from at least two concurrent causes: (1) Hypothyroidism — thyroid hormones are required for erythropoiesis; TSH 6.8 = undertreated hypothyroidism contributing to anemia via reduced erythroid progenitor stimulation; correcting TSH to normal range will improve Hgb; (2) CKD G3a anemia — EPO level 8.4 is inappropriately low for a Hgb of 10.1 (normal EPO response to anemia should be much higher). Priority: first optimize levothyroxine (increase dose to achieve TSH 0.5–2.0 mIU/L) and recheck Hgb in 8–12 weeks. If Hgb remains <10 after TSH correction and other causes excluded, then consider ESA discussion with nephrology. IDA (option A): ferritin 180 is not consistent with IDA — typically ferritin <12 is diagnostic of IDA. Osteoarthritis and NSAIDs (option C): she doesn't take NSAIDs and there's no GI blood loss marker.

questions.stemAnchor: Post-gastrectomy patient + B12 deficiency intrinsic factor — mechanism and treatment route
questions.stem: A 58-year-old man who underwent total gastrectomy for gastric cancer 3 years ago presents with fatigue, peripheral neuropathy, and cognitive slowing. B12 level is 98 pg/mL. His surgery removed the entire stomach including the fundus (where parietal cells produce intrinsic factor). He is not taking B12 supplements. Which of the following best describes why his B12 level is low and the most appropriate treatment route?

options.A: He cannot absorb oral B12 because intrinsic factor is absent post-gastrectomy — intramuscular B12 injections (1000 mcg IM) are required monthly
options.B: Oral B12 at standard dose (2.4 mcg/day) is sufficient — intrinsic factor is produced in the liver, not the stomach
options.C: Oral high-dose cyanocobalamin 1000–2000 mcg daily is effective — 1% of oral B12 is absorbed by passive diffusion regardless of intrinsic factor
options.D: IV B12 is required for neurological deficits — IM and oral routes are ineffective

questions.rationale: Post-total gastrectomy: parietal cells in the gastric fundus produce intrinsic factor (IF). Total gastrectomy removes all parietal cells → complete IF deficiency → no IF-mediated B12 absorption (the normal high-affinity mechanism). For IF deficiency: (1) Intramuscular cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg IM monthly is the traditional treatment and avoids GI absorption dependency; (2) High-dose oral B12 (1000–2000 mcg daily) via passive diffusion (1% passive absorption × 1000 mcg = 10 mcg/day — exceeds the daily requirement of 2–3 mcg) is actually an evidence-based alternative even in pernicious anemia/IF deficiency. However, given active neurological symptoms (peripheral neuropathy, cognitive slowing), the most reliable and appropriate approach is IM injections to ensure rapid repletion. Option C (high-dose oral) is theoretically valid and evidence-based but option A (IM) is more immediately reliable in a symptomatic patient with active neuropathy. The MOST appropriate answer for a symptomatic post-gastrectomy patient is IM injections.

questions.stemAnchor: Sideroblastic anemia + ringed sideroblasts + lead exposure + microcytic — lead poisoning recognition in adult
questions.stem: A 45-year-old male sheet metal worker presents with abdominal colic, constipation, fatigue, and wrist drop for 6 weeks. CBC: Hgb 9.8 g/dL, MCV 74 fL. Peripheral smear shows basophilic stippling. Bone marrow biopsy (done after initial workup was inconclusive) reveals ringed sideroblasts. He has a history of working with leaded paint and soldering materials without respiratory protection. Which of the following is the most important diagnostic test and management?

options.A: Measure serum B12 — basophilic stippling indicates megaloblastic process
options.B: Measure whole blood lead level — this presentation is consistent with lead poisoning (sideroblastic anemia + basophilic stippling + peripheral neuropathy + abdominal colic in an industrial worker)
options.C: Start iron replacement — microcytic anemia is IDA until proven otherwise
options.D: Order hemoglobin electrophoresis — thalassemia is the most common microcytic anemia

questions.rationale: Lead poisoning in adults: classic triad — (1) Microcytic anemia with basophilic stippling (lead inhibits ferrochelatase and ALA dehydratase → disrupts heme synthesis → iron accumulates in mitochondria around nucleus = ringed sideroblasts), (2) Abdominal colic (lead line on gingiva — Burton line, constipation, colicky pain), (3) Peripheral neuropathy (wrist drop = extensor compartment involvement — classic lead neuropathy). Occupational exposure (sheet metal work, soldering, leaded paint removal) confirms the exposure history. Diagnostic: WHOLE BLOOD lead level (NOT serum — lead distributes into RBCs). Management: remove from exposure; chelation therapy (succimer/DMSA for blood levels >45 mcg/dL in adults with symptoms, or dimercaprol + EDTA for severe toxicity). IDA (option C) would have low ferritin, not ringed sideroblasts. Thalassemia (option D) shows target cells and elevated HbA2, not basophilic stippling or neuropathy.

questions.stemAnchor: Anemia + phenytoin use + macrocytic — anticonvulsant folate deficiency mechanism
questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with epilepsy managed on phenytoin 300 mg daily for 15 years presents with fatigue and macrocytic anemia. CBC: Hgb 10.4 g/dL, MCV 102 fL. B12 level is 490 pg/mL (normal). Folate is 2.8 ng/mL (low). He eats a normal diet with vegetables and fortified foods. Which of the following best explains his folate deficiency?

options.A: Phenytoin causes direct bone marrow suppression and aplastic anemia
options.B: Phenytoin induces CYP450 enzymes that increase folate metabolism and also impairs intestinal folate absorption — this is a medication-induced folate deficiency; treat with folic acid 1 mg daily
options.C: Phenytoin causes macrocytosis through direct cell membrane effects unrelated to folate
options.D: Long-term phenytoin causes B12 deficiency despite a normal B12 level here

questions.rationale: Phenytoin-induced folate deficiency: (1) Phenytoin is a potent CYP450 inducer → accelerates folate metabolism and conversion; (2) Phenytoin impairs intestinal conjugase activity → reduces conversion of dietary polyglutamate folate to absorbable monoglutamate form. Together these mechanisms deplete folate despite adequate dietary intake. Other anticonvulsants that cause folate deficiency: carbamazepine, phenobarbital, primidone. Treatment: folic acid 1 mg daily. Caution: folic acid supplementation in epilepsy patients may reduce phenytoin levels (by competing for protein binding) and may theoretically lower seizure threshold — but the neurological risk of folate deficiency (neural tube defects if pregnancy, neuropathy) outweighs this concern. Always check B12 before treating macrocytic anemia with folate alone. Additional phenytoin-induced deficiency: Vitamin D (reduces absorption and accelerates metabolism → osteoporosis risk after years of use).
